questions.correct_feedback: Great! A mysterious package from your future self makes readers wonder what's inside. The author wants us curious and intrigued.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. A strange package sits for days and says it's from your future self. What feeling does that mystery give the reader?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is curious and intrigued. The mysterious package from Eliot's future self makes the reader wonder what's inside.

questions.prompt:
Everyone said the old house was haunted. Lina didn't believe in ghosts. She marched up the creaky steps, pushed open the door, and found... a family of raccoons living in the fireplace.

What makes this ending effective?
choices: It confirms the house is haunted
It surprises the reader with a funny, unexpected twist
questions.correct: It surprises the reader with a funny, unexpected twist
questions.hint: You expected something spooky, but got raccoons! That surprise is what makes it work.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Everyone expected ghosts, but Lina found raccoons instead. That surprising, funny twist is what makes the ending work.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Everyone thought ghosts, but Lina found something silly instead. What kind of ending catches the reader off guard?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that it surprises the reader with a funny, unexpected twist. Everyone expected ghosts, but it was raccoons.

questions.prompt:
Read the story.

Maya carried her painting home slowly, holding it flat so the wet paint would not run. Her little brother Sam ran up the sidewalk to meet her, waving a drawing he had made all by himself. "Look what I did!" he shouted, bumping right into her arm. Maya looked down at the long smear now streaking across her sky. She opened her mouth, then closed it. "Wow, Sam," she said, kneeling to look at his drawing. "Tell me about this part right here."

Why does Maya close her mouth before she speaks?
choices: She decides not to make Sam feel bad about the accident.
She is waiting for Sam to say he is sorry first.
She cannot think of anything nice to say about Sam's drawing.
She is too tired from carrying the painting to talk.
questions.correct: She decides not to make Sam feel bad about the accident.
questions.hint: Notice what Maya was about to do (open her mouth right after seeing the smear) versus what she actually chooses to do instead.
questions.correct_feedback: Well done! Maya was about to speak but chose kindness instead, deciding not to make Sam feel bad about the accident.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, Maya opens her mouth, then closes it and says something nice about Sam's drawing. Why might she hold back her first words?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that she decides not to make Sam feel bad about the accident. She holds back and chooses kind words instead.

questions.prompt:
Read the story.

The whole class had voted to name the class turtle "Rocket." Only Priya had wanted "Shelly." When Mr. Lee announced the winning name, Priya clapped along with everyone and helped tape the new name card to the tank. At recess, her friend Jack asked, "Aren't you upset your name lost?" Priya shrugged. "Everybody gets a say. That's how a class works," she said, and went to line up for kickball.

What does Priya's behavior most show about her?
choices: She changed her mind and now likes the name Rocket better.
She can accept a group decision even when it isn't what she wanted.
She never really cared what the turtle was named.
She was afraid the other students would laugh at her.
questions.correct: She can accept a group decision even when it isn't what she wanted.
questions.hint: Remember that Priya DID want a different name, then look at how she acts and what she tells Jack.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Priya wanted a different name but clapped and helped anyway. That shows she can accept a group decision even when she loses.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. Priya wanted Shelly but still helped and said everyone gets a say. What does that show about how she handles losing a vote?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that she can accept a group decision even when it isn't what she wanted. She wanted Shelly but supported the class.

questions.prompt:
Read the poem.

The old dog does not chase the ball,
He watches from the shady wall.
He knew each fence and every gate
Back when the pup was small and straight.
Now the pup runs, quick and new,
The old dog naps the afternoon through.

What is the poem mainly saying about the old dog and the pup?
choices: The pup is lost and the old dog is trying to find him.
The old dog and the pup do not like each other at all.
The pup is young and lively while the old dog is now slow with age.
The old dog is angry that the pup took his favorite ball.
questions.correct: The pup is young and lively while the old dog is now slow with age.
questions.hint: Compare the words used for the pup (quick, new) with what the old dog does now (watches, naps) across the whole poem.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! The old dog naps in the shade while the pup runs quick and new. The poem shows the pup is lively and the dog is now slow with age.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The old dog rests while the pup runs and plays. What is the poem mainly showing about these two dogs?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that the pup is young and lively while the old dog is now slow with age. The dog rests while the pup runs about.

questions.prompt:
Read the story.

Every morning, Grandpa set out two cups for tea, even though Grandma had passed away last spring. He would pour hot water into both, then sit quietly until one cup went cold. When Ravi came to visit, he noticed the second cup and started to ask about it, but Grandpa was already smiling out the window at the garden Grandma had planted. Ravi decided to pour himself some tea and sit down beside him.

Why does Grandpa most likely set out two cups each morning?
choices: He does not like to drink his tea until it has cooled off.
He forgets each day that Grandma has passed away.
He is expecting Ravi to come over for tea every morning.
He is remembering Grandma and keeping her close in his routine.
questions.correct: He is remembering Grandma and keeping her close in his routine.
questions.hint: Think about what one cup going cold, and Grandpa smiling at Grandma's garden, suggest together about his feelings.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Grandpa smiles at Grandma's garden and pours her cup each day. He's remembering her and keeping her close in his routine.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Grandma passed away, yet Grandpa still sets out her cup and smiles at her garden. Why might he keep doing this each morning?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that he is remembering Grandma and keeping her close in his routine. The second cup is a way to honor her each day.

questions.prompt:
Read the story.

Before the spelling bee, Omar had told everyone he would win easily. When he misspelled his very first word and had to sit down, he crossed his arms and muttered that the word wasn't fair. But that night, Omar quietly pulled out the word list and began writing each word three times. He taped the hardest ones to his bathroom mirror. He did not tell anyone what he was doing.

How does Omar change from the beginning of the story to the end?
choices: He stops bragging and quietly starts working to get better.
He becomes angrier at the judges as the day goes on.
He decides spelling bees are unfair and gives up on them.
He asks his friends to help him practice the word list.
questions.correct: He stops bragging and quietly starts working to get better.
questions.hint: Track Omar's actions in order: what he said at first, how he reacted after losing, and what he chose to do that night alone.
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! Omar bragged and blamed the word at first, but that night he quietly practiced. He stops bragging and starts working to get better.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Compare how Omar acts at the start with what he does that night. How is he different by the end?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that he stops bragging and quietly starts working to get better. At first he boasted, but that night he practiced alone.

standards.standard_description: Ask and answer questions to demonstrate understanding of a text, referring explicitly to the text.
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child to prove their answer by finding the exact sentence in the text.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: The Amazon Rainforest is the largest rainforest on our planet, stretching across many countries in South America. Scientists have found that it produces about 20 percent of the world's oxygen, which is why some people call it the lungs of the Earth. It is also an amazing home to more than 10 million different types of plants and animals, many of which live nowhere else. Because so many living things depend on it, protecting the Amazon is very important. Which statement tells two important things about the Amazon Rainforest, according to the text?
choices: It makes about 10 percent of the world's oxygen and has more than 20 million types of plants and animals.
It makes about 50 percent of the world's oxygen and has more than 10 million types of plants and animals.
It makes about 20 percent of the world's oxygen and has more than 10 million types of plants and animals.
It makes about 20 percent of the world's oxygen and has more than 5 million types of plants and animals.
questions.correct: It makes about 20 percent of the world's oxygen and has more than 10 million types of plants and animals.
questions.hint: Look at the first and second sentences of the passage to find both facts about the Amazon Rainforest.
questions.correct_feedback: Great listening! The text says the Amazon makes about 20 percent of the world's oxygen and is home to more than 10 million kinds of plants and animals.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, listen again for the two numbers in the text, the percent of oxygen and how many kinds of plants and animals. Match them both carefully.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that it makes about 20 percent of the world's oxygen and has more than 10 million types of plants and animals, just as the text said.

questions.prompt:
Harriet Tubman escaped slavery in 1849. She then risked her life many times to help others escape through the Underground Railroad. She led about 70 people to freedom.

How many people did Harriet Tubman help escape?
questions.hint: The last sentence gives you the exact number.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The text says Harriet Tubman led about 70 people to freedom through the Underground Railroad.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Go back to the text and find the exact number of people Harriet Tubman led to freedom. Read that part slowly.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is about 70. The text says Harriet Tubman led about 70 people to freedom through the Underground Railroad.

questions.prompt:
Earthquakes happen when large pieces of the Earth's crust, called tectonic plates, suddenly move. Most earthquakes occur along the edges of these plates.

What causes earthquakes?
questions.hint: The first sentence explains exactly what causes them.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The text explains that earthquakes happen when tectonic plates, big pieces of Earth's crust, suddenly move.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look at the text. It names one thing that suddenly moves and causes earthquakes. Find that cause in the words.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is tectonic plates suddenly moving. The text says earthquakes happen when these large pieces of Earth's crust suddenly move.

questions.prompt: According to the text, what did Thomas Edison say he found instead of failures when trying to invent the light bulb?
questions.hint: Refer to Edison's quote in the passage to see what he said he found.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Edison saw each try that failed as one more way that wouldn't work, so he kept going instead of giving up.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about how Edison felt about his mistakes. He didn't call them failures at all. What did he count instead?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "1,000 ways that won't work." Edison saw each miss as a lesson, not a failure, so he kept trying.

questions.prompt:
The International Space Station orbits Earth about every 90 minutes, traveling at 17,500 miles per hour. Astronauts aboard the station see 16 sunrises and sunsets every day.

Why do astronauts see 16 sunrises each day?
choices: The sun moves faster in space
The station orbits Earth so quickly
questions.correct: The station orbits Earth so quickly
questions.hint: It goes around Earth every 90 minutes — so it sees the sun rise and set with each orbit!
questions.correct_feedback: That's right! The station zooms around Earth so fast that it passes into day and night 16 times a day.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! There is only one sun. Think about how fast the station moves around Earth every 90 minutes.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that the station orbits Earth so quickly. Circling Earth every 90 minutes means many sunrises each day.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "Owls hunt at night. Their large eyes help them see in the dark." According to the text, when do owls hunt?
questions.hint: Look at the very first sentence. It tells you exactly when owls hunt.
questions.correct_feedback: Great reading! The text says owls hunt when it is dark, and their big eyes help them see.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Look back at the sentence and find the exact time of day it names for owls.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "At night." The text says owls hunt when it's dark, which is why their big eyes help.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "A camel can go many days without water. It stores fat in its hump." According to the text, where does a camel store fat?
questions.hint: The second sentence names the body part. Point to the word right before "hump."
questions.correct_feedback: Nice work! The text tells us a camel stores fat right in its hump.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Read the sentence again and find the body part where the camel keeps its fat.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "In its hump." The text says the camel stores fat there, which helps it go days without water.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "Bees make honey. They collect sweet nectar from flowers to do it." According to the text, what do bees make?
questions.hint: The first sentence says exactly what bees make. Reread it.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The text says bees make honey by collecting sweet nectar from flowers.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The sentence tells you exactly what bees make. What sweet thing does it name?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "Honey." The text says bees make it using the sweet nectar they gather from flowers.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "The Sahara is a very hot desert. It gets almost no rain all year." According to the text, how much rain does the Sahara get?
questions.hint: The last sentence tells you about the rain. Find the words that describe how much.
questions.correct_feedback: That's right! The text says the Sahara gets almost no rain all year, so it stays very dry.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. The Sahara is a very hot, dry desert. Read again to see how much rain it really gets.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "Almost no rain." The text says the Sahara gets barely any rain all year long.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "Penguins live where it is cold. They use their wings to swim instead of fly." According to the text, what do penguins use their wings for?
choices: To carry food in the sky
questions.hint: The second sentence tells what penguins do with their wings. Look for the word after "to."
questions.correct_feedback: Great job! The text says penguins use their wings to swim instead of to fly.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look back at the sentence. It tells you what penguins do with their wings in the water.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is "To swim." The text says penguins use their wings to move through water instead of flying.

standards.standard_description: Determine the main idea of a text; recount the key details and explain how they support the main idea.
standards.parent_tip: Ask 'What is this passage mostly about?' and 'What details help prove that main idea?'

questions.prompt:
Bees are essential to our food supply. They pollinate flowers that grow into fruits and vegetables. Without bees, we would lose about one-third of the food we eat.

What is the main idea?
choices: Bees are important for our food supply
questions.correct: Bees are important for our food supply
questions.hint: The first sentence states the main idea. The rest gives supporting details.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The whole passage is about how bees help grow our food, so that is the big main idea.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! One choice is a small detail. Ask yourself what ALL the sentences are mostly about.
questions.reveal_feedback: The main idea is that bees are important for our food supply. Every sentence supports how bees help our food grow.

questions.prompt:
Coral reefs are in danger. Pollution poisons the water. Rising temperatures cause coral to turn white and die. Overfishing removes animals that keep reefs healthy.

The key details all support the main idea that —
questions.hint: Pollution, rising temperatures, overfishing — these are all threats!
questions.correct_feedback: Right! Pollution, heat, and overfishing are all dangers, so the details show reefs face many threats.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Look at what pollution, heat, and overfishing all do to reefs. What do these details have in common?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that coral reefs face many threats. Pollution, heat, and overfishing are all dangers hurting the reefs.

questions.prompt: Read the passage below.Butterflies start as tiny eggs. The eggs hatch into larvae, which are called caterpillars. Caterpillars eat many leaves and grow bigger. After growing, a caterpillar forms a pupa around itself, often called a chrysalis. Inside the chrysalis, the caterpillar changes. Finally, a beautiful butterfly comes out!What is the main idea of this passage?
choices: Caterpillars eat many leaves to grow.
Butterflies go through different stages to grow.
A chrysalis is where a caterpillar changes.
questions.correct: Butterflies go through different stages to grow.
questions.hint: Reread the passage and think about what big idea all the sentences are teaching you.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The passage follows a butterfly from egg to caterpillar to chrysalis to butterfly, showing all its growing stages.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Some choices are just one step. Think about what the WHOLE passage, start to finish, is about.
questions.reveal_feedback: The main idea is that butterflies go through different stages to grow. The passage lists each step from egg to butterfly.

questions.prompt:
Ancient Egyptians invented many things we still use today. They created one of the first writing systems called hieroglyphics. They developed a 365-day calendar. They even invented toothpaste!

What is the main idea?
questions.hint: The passage lists several inventions. What big idea ties them together?
questions.correct_feedback: Great thinking! Writing, a calendar, and toothpaste are all inventions, so the big idea is that Egyptians were great inventors.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not quite yet. Writing, a calendar, and toothpaste are examples. What do all these examples show about the Egyptians?
questions.reveal_feedback: The main idea is that ancient Egyptians were great inventors. The writing, calendar, and toothpaste are all things they invented.

questions.prompt:
Maya's class wanted a garden, but they had no money and no tools. First, they sold their old books to earn some cash. Then each student brought one small shovel or rake from home. On Saturday, teachers AND parents came to help them dig. By spring, the garden was bursting with plants.

What is the main idea of this passage?
choices: The whole class worked together, step by step, to make their garden happen
Maya's class sold their old books to earn money
Spring is the best time to grow plants
questions.correct: The whole class worked together, step by step, to make their garden happen
questions.hint: The main idea is what ALL the sentences add up to, not just one thing that happened. Ask what the whole story is really about.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Selling books, bringing tools, and everyone helping shows the class worked together step by step to build the garden.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Some choices are just one step. What does the WHOLE story show about how the garden got made?
questions.reveal_feedback: The main idea is that the whole class worked together, step by step, to make their garden happen. Each detail is one of those steps.

questions.prompt:
Owls are built to hunt in the dark of night. Their huge eyes soak up even the tiniest bit of moonlight. The author also tells us that owls have soft, fluffy feathers.

How does the detail about soft feathers help support the main idea?
choices: Soft feathers help owls build better nests
Soft feathers let owls fly silently, so they can sneak up on prey at night
Soft feathers keep owls warm on cold nights
Soft feathers make owls look pretty and fluffy
questions.correct: Soft feathers let owls fly silently, so they can sneak up on prey at night
questions.hint: A detail supports the main idea only if it connects back to it. The main idea here is about HUNTING at night, so ask which choice ties feathers to hunting.
questions.correct_feedback: Sharp thinking! The passage is about hunting at night, and silent feathers help owls sneak up on prey in the dark.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the main idea is about hunting in the dark. Which choice connects soft feathers to catching prey at night?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that soft feathers let owls fly silently to sneak up on prey. That fits the main idea of hunting at night.

questions.prompt:
The Nile River gave ancient Egypt almost everything it needed. Its water let farmers grow wheat in the middle of a dry desert. When it flooded, it left behind thick mud that made rich soil for crops. Boats carried food and goods up and down the river so people could trade.

Which sentence best states the main idea?
choices: Boats can carry goods from place to place
The Nile River was very important to life in ancient Egypt
Rivers are found in many places around the world
questions.correct: The Nile River was very important to life in ancient Egypt
questions.hint: A good main idea is not too big and not too small. Cross out any choice that is way broader than the passage OR that is just one small detail from it.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Farming, rich soil, and trade all came from the river, so the Nile was very important to Egypt.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Farming, soil, and boats are all details. What do they all show about the Nile River for Egypt?
questions.reveal_feedback: The main idea is that the Nile River was very important to life in ancient Egypt. Each detail shows a way it helped.

questions.prompt:
Penguins have clever ways to stay warm in the freezing cold. They huddle together in huge groups so their bodies block the icy wind. They also have a thick layer of fat under their skin that holds in heat.

Which NEW sentence would best support the main idea of this passage?
choices: Baby penguins are covered in soft gray fluff
Penguins cannot fly up into the air like other birds
Penguins dive into the sea to catch fish to eat
Their feathers are packed tightly together to trap warm air against their skin
questions.correct: Their feathers are packed tightly together to trap warm air against their skin
questions.hint: The main idea is about how penguins STAY WARM. A supporting sentence must be about warmth, even if the other sentences are also true about penguins.
questions.correct_feedback: Great choice! Feathers trapping warm air is one more way penguins stay warm, so it fits the main idea perfectly.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The passage is about staying warm. Which new sentence gives ANOTHER way penguins keep in heat?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that their feathers trap warm air against their skin. Like huddling and fat, it's one more way to stay warm.

questions.prompt:
Many people think spiders are creepy pests they should squash. But spiders actually help us in ways we hardly notice. They catch flies and mosquitoes that carry germs. They eat the bugs that would chew holes in our garden plants. Some farmers even count on spiders to protect their crops.

What is the main idea of this passage?
choices: Spiders are actually helpful to people
Mosquitoes carry germs that can make people sick
Spiders are creepy pests that people should squash
Farmers work hard to grow their crops
questions.correct: Spiders are actually helpful to people
questions.hint: Watch the word 'But' after the first sentence. Sometimes the first line says what people WRONGLY think, and the real main idea is the opposite.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Catching germ-carrying bugs and protecting crops all show that spiders are actually helpful to people.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. One choice is what people WRONGLY think. What does the passage prove spiders really do for us?
questions.reveal_feedback: The main idea is that spiders are actually helpful to people. They eat harmful bugs and even protect farmers' crops.

questions.prompt: Read this paragraph: "Owls are great hunters at night. They have big eyes that see in the dark. They have sharp ears that hear tiny sounds. They have quiet wings that make no noise when they fly." What is the main idea?
choices: The moon is bright at night
questions.hint: Look at the very first sentence. It tells you what the whole paragraph is about.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Big eyes, sharp ears, and quiet wings all help owls hunt at night, so that is the main idea.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Every sentence names a way owls hunt in the dark. What are all the clues mostly about?
questions.reveal_feedback: The main idea is that owls are great night hunters. Their eyes, ears, and wings all help them hunt in the dark.

questions.prompt: Read this paragraph: "Plants need many things to grow. They need water from the rain. They need sunlight from the sky. They need good soil for their roots." What is the main idea?
choices: Plants need many things to grow
questions.correct: Plants need many things to grow
questions.hint: The first sentence tells you the big idea. The other sentences list the details.
questions.correct_feedback: Great job! Water, sunlight, and good soil are all things plants need, so that is the big idea.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The paragraph lists water, sunlight, and soil. What do all these things have to do with plants?
questions.reveal_feedback: The main idea is that plants need many things to grow. Water, sunlight, and soil are all things they need.

questions.prompt: Read this paragraph: "Dogs help people in many ways. Some dogs guide people who cannot see. Some dogs find people who are lost. Some dogs keep homes safe." What is the main idea?
choices: Cats like to nap all day
Dogs help people in many ways
questions.correct: Dogs help people in many ways
questions.hint: Which choice sums up ALL the details about what dogs do? Check the first sentence.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Guiding, finding lost people, and keeping homes safe all show that dogs help people in many ways.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Every sentence tells a way dogs help. What is the whole paragraph mostly about?
questions.reveal_feedback: The main idea is that dogs help people in many ways. Guiding, finding, and guarding are all ways they help.

questions.prompt: Read this paragraph: "The library is a busy place. Kids read books at the tables. A teacher reads a story out loud. People use the computers to learn." What is the main idea?
choices: The library is a busy place
Books can be heavy to carry
questions.correct: The library is a busy place
questions.hint: All the details show people doing things. What one sentence covers them all?
questions.correct_feedback: Nice work! Kids reading, a teacher reading aloud, and people on computers all show the library is a busy place.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Every sentence tells something happening at the library. What is the whole paragraph mostly about?
questions.reveal_feedback: The main idea is that the library is a busy place. Reading, storytime, and computers all show how busy it is.

questions.prompt: Read this paragraph: "Winter can be very cold. People wear thick coats to stay warm. Snow covers the ground. Ponds freeze into hard ice." What is the main idea?
questions.hint: The details are all about cold weather. The first sentence names the main idea.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Thick coats, snow, and frozen ponds all show that winter can be very cold.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Coats, snow, and ice are all clues. What season and feeling are all these details about?
questions.reveal_feedback: The main idea is that winter can be very cold. Coats, snow, and frozen ponds all show the cold.

standards.standard_description: Describe the relationship between historical events, scientific ideas, or steps in technical procedures using time, sequence, and cause/effect language.
standards.parent_tip: Help your child spot signal words like 'because,' 'as a result,' 'first,' 'then,' 'finally.'

questions.prompt:
Because the river flooded, farmers lost their crops. As a result, food prices went up that year.

What caused food prices to go up?
choices: Farmers lost crops due to flooding
questions.correct: Farmers lost crops due to flooding
questions.hint: Follow the chain: flood → lost crops → higher food prices.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The flood made farmers lose crops, and less food made prices go up. You followed the cause!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, read the first sentence again. What happened to the crops that led to prices rising?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that farmers lost crops due to flooding. Fewer crops meant less food, so prices went up.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: In 1955, in the city of Montgomery, a woman named Rosa Parks was riding home on a crowded bus. When the driver told her to give up her seat, she quietly refused, and she was arrested for it. The news of her brave choice spread quickly through the community. In response, thousands of people decided to stop riding the city buses altogether, and this boycott of the buses lasted for many months. What happened because Rosa Parks refused to give up her bus seat?
choices: The Supreme Court ruled bus segregation was unconstitutional.
People started a boycott of the city buses.
Many people started riding buses more often.
Rosa Parks was given a new bus seat.
questions.correct: People started a boycott of the city buses.
questions.hint: Look at what event immediately followed Rosa Parks' refusal in the passage to understand the direct relationship.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! After Rosa Parks refused, thousands of people stopped riding the buses, starting a long boycott.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Read what thousands of people decided to do after her brave choice. What did they stop doing?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that people started a boycott of the city buses. After she refused, thousands stopped riding for months.

questions.prompt:
After the Great Fire of London in 1666, the city was rebuilt with stone and brick instead of wood. This made buildings much safer from future fires.

How are these two events connected?
choices: The stone buildings caused the fire
The fire caused the city to change how it built buildings
London stopped building after the fire
questions.correct: The fire caused the city to change how it built buildings
questions.hint: The fire was the cause. Rebuilding with stone was the effect.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Because of the fire, London rebuilt with stone and brick to stay safer from future fires.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Think about why London switched from wood to stone. What event made the city change how it built?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that the fire caused the city to change how it built. After it, London used stone and brick to be safer.

questions.prompt:
Mangrove trees grow along tropical coastlines. Their roots extend into the water and slow down waves. Due to this, mangroves prevent erosion and protect the shore during storms.

What is the EFFECT of mangrove roots slowing down waves?
choices: The shore is protected from erosion
questions.correct: The shore is protected from erosion
questions.hint: Look for the signal phrase 'due to this' — what comes after it is the effect!
questions.correct_feedback: Great thinking! Slower waves can't wear away as much land, so the shore is protected from erosion.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! When the roots slow the waves down, what does that DO for the shore? Read the last sentence again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that the shore is protected from erosion. Slowing the waves keeps them from wearing away the land.

questions.prompt:
To study whale migration, scientists first attach a small tracking device to the whale. Next, they monitor the whale's location using satellites. Then, they record the data over several months. Finally, they compare routes from different years.

What do scientists do IMMEDIATELY AFTER attaching the tracking device?
choices: Monitor the whale's location using satellites
questions.correct: Monitor the whale's location using satellites
questions.hint: Look for the word 'Next' — it tells you what comes right after the first step!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The steps say next they monitor the whale with satellites, so that comes right after the device goes on.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look at the order. After attaching the device, what does the word "next" tell scientists to do?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that they monitor the whale using satellites. The passage says that is the very next step.

questions.prompt:
Plastic breaks down into tiny pieces called microplastics. Since microplastics are so small, fish mistake them for food and eat them. Consequently, the chemicals in the plastic enter the food chain.

What causes the chemicals to enter the food chain?
questions.hint: Follow the chain: plastic becomes tiny, fish eat it, then the chemicals enter the food chain!
questions.correct_feedback: Right! When fish mistake microplastics for food and eat them, the chemicals move into the food chain.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Trace how the plastic gets INTO the food chain. What do the fish do that starts that?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that fish eat the microplastics. Once fish swallow them, the chemicals enter the food chain.

questions.prompt:
After a hurricane passes over the ocean, the strong winds push huge waves onto the shore. These waves carry sand, shells, and debris inland. In the following weeks, the beach slowly rebuilds as calmer waves bring sand back.

What happens FIRST in this process?
choices: A hurricane's winds push waves onto shore
questions.correct: A hurricane's winds push waves onto shore
questions.hint: Read the passage from the beginning — what event starts the whole process?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The passage starts with the hurricane's winds pushing waves onto shore, so that happens first.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Read the order of events. Which one happens at the very beginning, before the beach rebuilds?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that a hurricane's winds push waves onto shore. That is the first step, before the beach slowly rebuilds.

questions.prompt:
The ground got very cold. Because of the cold, the water in the pond turned to ice.

What caused the water to turn to ice?
choices: A duck landed on the pond
questions.hint: Look for the word "Because." It points right to the cause.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The cold ground made the pond water freeze into ice. You found the cause!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, read again. The words "because of the cold" are a clue. What got cold and froze the water?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that the ground got very cold. The cold is what turned the pond water into ice.

questions.prompt:
First, the seed was planted in the soil. Next, it was watered every day. Then, a small green plant grew.

What happened FIRST?
choices: The seed was planted in the soil
questions.correct: The seed was planted in the soil
questions.hint: Find the step that starts with the word "First."
questions.correct_feedback: Great job! The word "first" tells us the seed was planted before it was watered or grew.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Look for the word "first" in the story. What happened at the very start?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that the seed was planted in the soil. The word "first" shows that step came before the others.

questions.prompt:
Maria forgot her umbrella. Because of that, she got wet in the rain.

Why did Maria get wet?
choices: She jumped in a puddle on purpose
questions.hint: The words "Because of that" tell you the reason.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Maria forgot her umbrella, so she had nothing to keep the rain off and got wet.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The words "because of that" are a clue. What did Maria forget that led to her getting wet?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that she forgot her umbrella. With no umbrella, the rain got her wet.

questions.prompt:
The volcano erupted. After the eruption, hot lava flowed down the mountain.

What happened AFTER the volcano erupted?
choices: Hot lava flowed down the mountain
questions.correct: Hot lava flowed down the mountain
questions.hint: Look for the word "After" to find what came next.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! After the volcano erupted, hot lava flowed down the mountain.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The word "after" is a clue. What happened once the volcano erupted?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that hot lava flowed down the mountain. That happened right after the eruption.

questions.prompt:
To make toast, you first put bread in the toaster. Then you push the lever down.

What do you do FIRST to make toast?
questions.hint: The word "first" shows you the very first step.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The word "first" tells us you put bread in the toaster before pushing the lever.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Look for the word "first" in the steps. What do you do before pushing the lever down?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is put bread in the toaster. The word "first" shows that step comes before pushing the lever.

standards.standard_description: Determine the meaning of general academic and domain-specific words and phrases in a text.
standards.parent_tip: Help your child use context clues and word parts to figure out subject-specific vocabulary.

questions.prompt: Imagine a plant soaking up sunshine! It's also drinking water and taking in a special gas from the air. All of these things help the plant do its job of making food for itself. This important job is called photosynthesis.
choices: How plants make their own food.
A type of gas plants breathe.
questions.correct: How plants make their own food.
questions.hint: Look for the sentence that connects 'this important job' to what the plant is doing.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Sunlight, water, and gas all help the plant make its own food, which is called photosynthesis.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Drinking water and getting sunlight are just parts. What ONE big job do they all help the plant do?
questions.reveal_feedback: The passage is mostly about how plants make their own food. Sunlight, water, and gas all help do that job.

questions.prompt:
The Constitution is the supreme law of the United States. It establishes the structure of the government and protects citizens" rights.

What does **supreme** mean in this context?
questions.hint: The Constitution is the law that all other laws must follow — it's the top one!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The Constitution is called the supreme law, meaning it is the highest and most important one.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The text calls it the supreme law that everything follows. What would "supreme" mean about it?
questions.reveal_feedback: "Supreme" means highest or most important. The Constitution is the top law that all others must follow.

questions.prompt:
Erosion is the gradual wearing away of land by water, wind, or ice. Over thousands of years, erosion carved the Grand Canyon.

What does **gradual** mean?
questions.hint: It took THOUSANDS of years. That's definitely not fast!
questions.correct_feedback: Right! The canyon formed over thousands of years, so gradual means happening slowly over time.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The clue is that erosion took thousands of years. Would that be fast or slow?
questions.reveal_feedback: "Gradual" means happening slowly over time. It took thousands of years of erosion to carve the Grand Canyon.

questions.prompt: Deer, rabbits, and cows are called herbivores. These animals eat things like grass, clover, and hay. Based on this information, what do herbivores eat?
questions.hint: Think about the food examples given for deer, rabbits, and cows.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The text says herbivores eat grass, clover, and hay, and those are all plants.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Grass, clover, and hay are the examples given. What kind of food are all of those?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is only plants. Grass, clover, and hay are all plants, which is what herbivores eat.

questions.prompt:
The expedition to the South Pole took months of preparation. The team packed food, fuel, and navigation equipment for the dangerous journey.

What is an **expedition**?
choices: A long journey for a specific purpose
questions.correct: A long journey for a specific purpose
questions.hint: It took months of preparation for a dangerous journey to the South Pole.
questions.correct_feedback: Great job! A trip to the South Pole that took months of planning is a long journey for a special purpose.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the team spent months getting ready for a dangerous trip. What would an "expedition" be?
questions.reveal_feedback: An "expedition" is a long journey for a specific purpose, like the months-long trip to the South Pole.

questions.prompt:
Read this science passage:

"Salmon are strong swimmers. When it is time to lay their eggs, they must travel up the river to the place where they were born. Even when the river's current pushes hard against them, the salmon keep swimming forward and never give up."

What does the word **current** mean in this passage?
choices: The flow of water moving in a direction
The newest and most modern kind
Something that is happening right now
questions.correct: The flow of water moving in a direction
questions.hint: The word has more than one meaning. Look at what the current DOES to the salmon in the river to pick the right one.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The river pushes against the salmon, so current means the flow of water moving in a direction.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The word has many meanings. Use the passage: something in the river pushes against the fish. What is that?
questions.reveal_feedback: Here "current" means the flow of water moving in a direction. It's what pushes against the swimming salmon.

questions.prompt:
Read this passage:

"In winter, a bear does not die. It curls up in its cave for a long, deep sleep. Its heartbeat slows down, and it does not eat a single bite for many months. Scientists say the bear stays dormant until spring warms the air."

What does **dormant** mean here?
choices: Awake and moving around the cave
Very hungry and hunting for food
questions.hint: Each wrong answer matches ONE small detail. Find the meaning that fits the WHOLE picture the passage paints.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! The bear's heartbeat slows and it doesn't eat for months, so dormant means resting and not active.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The bear is in a long, deep sleep, not dead. What does dormant mean if it wakes in spring?
questions.reveal_feedback: "Dormant" means resting and not active. The bear sleeps deeply all winter, then wakes when spring comes.

questions.prompt:
Read this passage:

"The desert fox has huge, wide ears. On a blazing hot day, extra heat leaves the fox's body right through those big ears. This clever trick helps the fox keep its cool while the sun beats down on the sand."

What does **keep its cool** mean in this passage?
choices: Hide in the shade from other animals
Keep its body from getting too hot
Stay calm and not feel scared
questions.correct: Keep its body from getting too hot
questions.hint: This phrase can mean 'stay calm' in everyday talk, but check what the fox's ears are actually doing in the passage.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Heat leaves through the fox's big ears, so keep its cool means keep its body from getting too hot.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The passage is about a blazing hot day and losing heat. What does keeping cool mean here?
questions.reveal_feedback: "Keep its cool" here means keep its body from getting too hot. Heat leaves through the fox's big ears.

questions.prompt:
Read this passage:

"Most metals are stiff. If you try to bend them too far, they will snap in two. But copper is different. You can bend a copper wire into a circle, twist it around, and straighten it out again, all without breaking it. Copper is very flexible."

What does **flexible** mean here?
choices: Quick to snap into two pieces
Hard and stiff like most metals
questions.hint: The word 'but' signals that copper is the OPPOSITE of the other metals. Use that contrast to find the meaning.
questions.correct_feedback: Great thinking! You can bend copper wire without it snapping, so flexible means easy to bend without breaking.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. The passage says copper bends and twists without snapping. What would flexible mean?
questions.reveal_feedback: "Flexible" means easy to bend without breaking. Unlike stiff metals, copper bends and twists and stays whole.

questions.prompt:
Read this passage:

"A sponge is full of tiny holes you can barely see. Set it down in a puddle of spilled milk, and the milk seems to vanish. It slips into all those little holes and stays there, so the sponge can absorb the whole spill before it drips off the table."

What does **absorb** mean in this passage?
choices: To soak up and hold inside
To make the liquid dry up in the air
To wipe the table until it shines
questions.correct: To soak up and hold inside
questions.hint: Follow where the milk goes in the passage. What happens to it because of the sponge's tiny holes?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The sponge soaks the milk into its little holes and holds it, so absorb means to soak up and hold inside.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The milk slips into the sponge's tiny holes and stays. What does absorb mean from that clue?
questions.reveal_feedback: "Absorb" means to soak up and hold inside. The sponge pulls the milk into its tiny holes and keeps it there.

questions.prompt:
The bakery was famous for its enormous cakes. Each one was so big that ten kids could share a single slice!

In this text, what does the word **enormous** mean?
questions.hint: The sentence says the cakes were so BIG that ten kids could share one slice. Which choice means big?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The cakes were so big ten kids shared one slice, so enormous means very large.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The clue is that ten kids could share one slice. Would that cake be big or small?
questions.reveal_feedback: "Enormous" means very large. The cakes were so big that ten kids could share just one slice.

questions.prompt:
Sea turtles begin their life on a sandy beach. When the baby turtles hatch from their eggs, they crawl toward the water.

In this text, what does the word **hatch** mean?
choices: To come out of an egg
questions.correct: To come out of an egg
questions.hint: The sentence says the baby turtles hatch FROM THEIR EGGS. What are the babies doing with the eggs?
questions.correct_feedback: Great job! Baby turtles hatch from their eggs, so hatch means to come out of an egg.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The text says the baby turtles hatch from their eggs. What does that tell you hatch means?
questions.reveal_feedback: "Hatch" means to come out of an egg. The baby sea turtles hatch, then crawl toward the water.

questions.prompt:
The desert is a very dry place. Almost no rain falls there, so the ground stays dusty all year long.

In this text, what does the word **dry** mean?
questions.hint: The text says almost no rain falls in the desert. What does that tell you about water?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The text says almost no rain falls and the ground stays dusty, so dry means having very little water. Nice reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look again at the words 'almost no rain falls.' What would that do to the ground?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Having very little water.' The desert gets almost no rain, so dry means there is barely any water.

questions.prompt:
Bees are very helpful insects. They gather sweet nectar from flowers and use it to make honey.

In this text, what does the word **gather** mean?
questions.hint: The bees GATHER nectar from flowers to make honey. What are they doing with the nectar? They are picking it up and collecting it.
questions.correct_feedback: Great job! The bees take nectar from flowers to make honey, so gather means to collect. You used the clues!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Reread how the bees take nectar from flowers. What are they doing with it?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'To collect.' The bees pick up nectar from flowers, and gather means to collect it together.

questions.prompt:
A firefighter's job is dangerous. They rush into burning buildings, so they must be very brave.

In this text, what does the word **brave** mean?
questions.hint: The text says firefighters rush INTO burning buildings. Someone who does that scary job is not afraid.
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! They rush into burning buildings, so brave means not afraid. Smart thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Think about someone who runs into a burning building. How must they feel to do that?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Not afraid.' Firefighters rush into danger, so brave means they are not afraid.

standards.standard_description: Use text features and search tools to locate information relevant to a given topic efficiently.
standards.parent_tip: Practice using a table of contents, index, headings, and search bars to find information quickly.

questions.prompt: Imagine you have a science textbook and you want to quickly find all the pages that explain what volcanoes are. Which text feature would be the most helpful to use?
questions.hint: Think about which text feature lists many different topics in alphabetical order and tells you every page where that topic is mentioned in the book.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The index lists topics with their page numbers, so it quickly points you to every page about volcanoes. Well done!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Which part lists topics with the exact page numbers where you can find them?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The index.' It lists topics in order with page numbers, so you can quickly find every volcano page.

questions.prompt: You're researching dolphins online. Which search term would give you the BEST results?
questions.hint: Specific keywords help you find exactly what you need!
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! Clear, exact words like 'dolphin habitat and diet' tell the computer just what you want, so you get the best results.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Which search uses clear, exact words about dolphins instead of vague ones?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Dolphin habitat and diet.' Exact words tell the computer exactly what you want, giving the best results.

questions.prompt: Imagine you are reading a non-fiction book about space and you want to quickly find the exact page that talks about "Mars." Which part of the book would you use to efficiently locate this specific information?
questions.hint: Look for the part of the text that explains which feature lists specific topics and their page numbers, usually found at the back of the book.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The index lists topics with page numbers, so you can find the exact Mars page fast. Great work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, which part of a book lists topics with the exact page number for each one?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The index.' It lists topics with page numbers, so you can quickly find the exact page about Mars.

questions.prompt: Hyperlinks in a digital text allow you to —
questions.hint: Clicking a hyperlink takes you to another page or section with more information!
questions.correct_feedback: Right! A hyperlink is like a doorway you click to jump straight to more information. Nice thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Think about what happens when you click a link on a screen. Where does it take you?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Jump to related information quickly.' A hyperlink is a link you click to go straight to more about that topic.

questions.prompt: A nonfiction book about sharks has a list at the very front that shows chapter names like "What Sharks Eat" and the page each one starts on. What is this list called?
questions.hint: It's the list at the front that names the chapters and tells you what page each one begins on.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A list of chapter names with their starting pages at the front is the table of contents. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! What do we call the list at the front that shows chapter names and their pages?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The table of contents.' It sits at the front and lists chapter names with the page each one starts on.

questions.prompt: You are reading about spiders. You see a bold word you don't know: "venom." Which part of the book would tell you what "venom" means?
questions.hint: The glossary is the part of the book that gives the meanings of important words.
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! A glossary is like a mini dictionary in the book that tells you what tricky words mean. Well done!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Which part is like a little dictionary that explains what words mean?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The glossary.' It works like a small dictionary in the book that tells you what words like venom mean.

questions.prompt: You are typing a search on the computer because you want to learn what pandas eat. Which words should you type to get the best help?
questions.hint: Pick the words that clearly say exactly what you want to find out.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Clear, exact words like 'what do pandas eat' tell the computer just what you want to learn. Great searching!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Which choice uses clear words that ask exactly what you want to know about pandas?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'What do pandas eat.' These clear words tell the computer exactly what you want, so you get the best help.

questions.prompt: In a book about the ocean, the words at the top of a section say "How Whales Breathe." What is this called, and what does it tell you?
choices: A glossary, which tells you the author
A page number, which tells you the date
A cover, which tells you the price
A heading, which tells you what that section is about
questions.correct: A heading, which tells you what that section is about
questions.hint: The bold words at the top of a section are a heading, and they tell you what you'll read about there.
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! A heading sits at the top of a section and tells you what that part is about. Smart!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, words at the top of a section that name what it's about have a special name. Which choice matches that job?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'A heading, which tells you what that section is about.' The words at the top name the topic of that part.

questions.prompt: You want to find just the pages that talk about "rain" in a big weather book. There is a list at the back that names topics in ABC order with the pages for each one. What is that list called?
questions.hint: The list at the back in ABC order that points you to exact pages for a topic is the index.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A list of topics in ABC order with page numbers at the back is the index. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Which part is at the back and lists topics in ABC order with their pages?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The index.' It sits at the back and lists topics in ABC order with the pages for each one.

standards.standard_description: Distinguish their own point of view from that of the author of a text.
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child 'What does the author think about this topic? Do you agree or disagree?'

questions.prompt:
After a long day at school, some kids just want to play. They might think homework is just more work. But doing a little practice at home can really help your brain remember new things. If you practice your sight words, you'll read faster. If you review your math problems, you'll feel ready for the test. Homework gives your brain a chance to grow even stronger and helps you truly understand what you learned in class.

The author of this passage most likely believes that homework is:
choices: only for when you have extra time to play.
something teachers give just to be mean.
helpful for making your brain stronger and smarter.
boring and just extra work after school.
questions.correct: helpful for making your brain stronger and smarter.
questions.hint: Look for what the author says homework *does* for your brain and learning in the passage.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author says practice helps your brain remember and grow stronger, so the author thinks homework is helpful. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Reread how the author says homework helps your brain. Does the author sound for it or against it?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'helpful for making your brain stronger and smarter.' The author says practice helps your brain grow and remember.

questions.prompt: A student reads an article that says school should start later. The student likes waking up early. This shows —
choices: The student and author have different points of view
questions.correct: The student and author have different points of view
questions.hint: It's okay to disagree with an author! That's having your own point of view.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! The article wants a later start, but the student likes early mornings, so they see it differently. Nice thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The article and the student want different things. What does that tell us about how they think?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The student and author have different points of view.' The article wants a later start, but the student likes early.

questions.prompt:
Read the paragraph. What is the author's point of view about dogs?

"Dogs are the best pets anyone could ask for! They are always so happy to see you when you come home, wagging their tails. Dogs love to play fetch in the park, which is great exercise for both of you. Plus, they can learn tricks like 'sit' and 'stay' very quickly. Having a dog means you always have a loyal friend by your side."
choices: Dogs mostly like to stay inside.
Dogs are sometimes hard to train.
Dogs are wonderful pets to own.
Dogs need a lot of healthy food.
questions.correct: Dogs are wonderful pets to own.
questions.hint: Look for the sentences that tell you how the author feels about dogs in the paragraph.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author lists only good things about dogs, so the point of view is that dogs are wonderful pets. Well done!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, notice all the good things the author says about dogs. How does the author feel about them overall?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Dogs are wonderful pets to own.' The author only shares good things, showing they think dogs are great.

questions.prompt:
Zoos keep animals in small spaces where they can't roam freely. Animals deserve to live in their natural habitats.

The author most likely believes —
choices: Zoos aren't the best place for animals
questions.correct: Zoos aren't the best place for animals
questions.hint: Words like 'small spaces' and 'deserve to live in natural habitats' show the author's opinion.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! The author says animals deserve their natural homes, so they think zoos aren't the best place. Good thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The author says animals deserve their natural habitats. How does that make them feel about zoos?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Zoos aren't the best place for animals.' The author says animals deserve their natural homes, not small spaces.

questions.prompt: After reading the text, "Eating a big salad every single night is the best way to stay strong and smart. Only vegetables can give you all the vitamins you need. If you don't eat a salad, you might not be as healthy as you could be," which statement shows a different point of view you might have about staying healthy?
choices: The author is correct that everyone should eat salads every night.
Eating a big salad every night is the only way to be healthy.
Salads are good, but I think other healthy foods like fruit or milk are also important for being strong.
It doesn't matter what you eat, as long as you exercise a lot.
questions.correct: Salads are good, but I think other healthy foods like fruit or milk are also important for being strong.
questions.hint: Think about what the author says is the *only* and *best* way to be healthy, and then consider what *you* know about staying healthy.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! You added your own idea that other healthy foods matter too, so that's a different point of view. Great!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! A different point of view means your own idea, not just agreeing with the author. Which one shares a new idea?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Salads are good, but I think other healthy foods like fruit or milk are also important.' It adds your own view.

questions.prompt: An author writes: "Every school should have a garden. Growing vegetables teaches kids patience and shows them where food really comes from. A plain, boring yard can become a place of wonder." Jordan reads this and thinks a garden sounds like too much dirty, sweaty work. What does this show?
choices: The author likes school gardens, but Jordan does not want the work
The author thinks gardens are too much work
The author and Jordan both dislike school gardens
Jordan agrees that gardens teach kids patience
questions.correct: The author likes school gardens, but Jordan does not want the work
questions.hint: Figure out how the AUTHOR feels first, then how Jordan feels. Do their feelings match?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author loves school gardens, but Jordan doesn't want the messy work, so their views differ. Sharp reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The author wants gardens, but how does Jordan feel about all that dirty work?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The author likes school gardens, but Jordan does not want the work.' They feel differently about gardens.

questions.prompt: Read this part of an article about bats: "Bats are the only mammals that can truly fly. They use sounds we cannot hear to find insects at night. One little brown bat can eat thousands of bugs in a single hour. Without a doubt, bats are the most amazing animals in the whole world." Which sentence shows the AUTHOR'S opinion, not a fact?
choices: One little brown bat can eat thousands of bugs in a single hour.
Without a doubt, bats are the most amazing animals in the whole world.
Bats are the only mammals that can truly fly.
They use sounds we cannot hear to find insects at night.
questions.correct: Without a doubt, bats are the most amazing animals in the whole world.
questions.hint: A fact can be checked and proven. An opinion is what the writer believes or feels. Which sentence could someone disagree with?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'The most amazing animals in the whole world' is what the author feels, not something you can prove. Great catch!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. An opinion is a feeling, not something you can check or count. Which sentence is a feeling?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Without a doubt, bats are the most amazing animals in the whole world.' That's a feeling, not a fact.

questions.prompt: An author writes: "Zoos are wonderful places. They let families see amazing animals up close, and they help protect animals that are in danger. Every city should build one!" Priya reads this. She agrees that zoos help protect animals, but it makes her sad to see animals living in cages. How is Priya's point of view DIFFERENT from the author's?
choices: Priya thinks every city should build a zoo
Priya and the author feel exactly the same way about zoos
Priya agrees zoos protect animals but feels they can also be sad places
Priya disagrees that zoos help protect animals
questions.correct: Priya agrees zoos protect animals but feels they can also be sad places
questions.hint: Priya agrees with ONE thing the author said and disagrees with another. Find the part where they don't match.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Priya agrees zoos protect animals but feels sad seeing cages, so her view is different. Thoughtful reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, Priya agrees about one thing but feels differently about another. Which choice shows both parts?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Priya agrees zoos protect animals but feels they can also be sad places.' She shares the author's view only partway.

questions.prompt: Two kids wrote about the SAME field trip to the farm. Sam wrote: "The barn smelled terrible, my shoes got covered in mud, and it rained the whole time." Dana wrote: "We fed baby goats, picked fresh red apples, and learned how cows are milked." Even though they went on the same trip, what can you tell?
choices: Dana must have gone on a different trip than Sam
Sam and Dana felt the same way about the trip
The field trip was mostly a bad experience
The two writers had different feelings about the trip
questions.correct: The two writers had different feelings about the trip
questions.hint: Look at WHICH details each writer chose to share. What do their choices tell you about how each one felt?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Sam noticed bad things and Dana noticed fun things, so they had different feelings about the same trip. Nice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Sam and Dana went to the same farm but wrote very different things. What does that tell you about their feelings?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The two writers had different feelings about the trip.' Sam saw the bad, Dana saw the fun.

questions.prompt: An author writes that video games are bad for kids because they waste time that could be spent reading books. Leo loves video games. He thinks they help him solve tricky puzzles and make friends online. If Leo wrote a note back to the author, which sentence would BEST show LEO'S own point of view?
choices: Games have taught me to solve problems and work with friends.
Kids should read more books instead of playing games.
You are right that games just waste kids' time.
Video games are bad for children.
questions.correct: Games have taught me to solve problems and work with friends.
questions.hint: Three of these sentences match what the AUTHOR believes. Pick the one that shows what LEO believes.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Leo's own view is that games help him solve problems and make friends. That sentence is truly his idea. Great!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Leo's point of view is his own feeling about games, not the author's. Which sentence sounds like Leo?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Games have taught me to solve problems and work with friends.' That shares Leo's own point of view.

standards.standard_description: Use information from illustrations and the words in a text to demonstrate understanding.
standards.parent_tip: Point out maps, diagrams, and charts in nonfiction — ask what new information they provide.

questions.prompt: A science text describes the water cycle. Beside it is a diagram with arrows showing evaporation, condensation, and precipitation. The diagram helps by —
choices: Showing the process visually so it's easier to understand
questions.correct: Showing the process visually so it's easier to understand
questions.hint: Diagrams show you HOW something works in a way words alone can't.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The diagram with arrows shows the steps, so it helps you see the process and understand it more easily. Well done!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The diagram uses arrows to show each step. How does seeing the steps help a reader?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Showing the process visually so it's easier to understand.' The picture and arrows make the steps clear to see.

questions.prompt: A map in a history book shows the route Lewis and Clark traveled. The map helps readers understand —
choices: How far and where they traveled
questions.correct: How far and where they traveled
questions.hint: Maps show locations and distances!
questions.correct_feedback: Right! A map shows the path and distance, so it helps readers understand how far and where they traveled. Nice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about what a map shows best. Does it show places and paths, or things like food and birthdays?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'How far and where they traveled.' A map shows the route and distance of the journey.

questions.prompt: A photograph in a textbook shows a volcano erupting with lava flowing down its side. The photograph adds to the text by —
choices: Showing what an eruption actually looks like
questions.correct: Showing what an eruption actually looks like
questions.hint: Photos show real-world images that help you picture what the text describes.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A photograph shows the real thing, so it shows what an erupting volcano actually looks like. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! A photo shows a real picture, not words or explanations. What does the photo let you see?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Showing what an eruption actually looks like.' A photograph shows the real thing to the reader.

questions.prompt: A bar graph in a text about favorite school lunches shows pizza at 45%, tacos at 30%, and salad at 25%. What information does the graph give that the text might not?
choices: The exact percentages at a glance
questions.correct: The exact percentages at a glance
questions.hint: Graphs let you quickly SEE and COMPARE numbers!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A bar graph lets you see the exact percentages quickly with just one look. Smart!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. A graph shows numbers in a picture. What does it let you spot quickly that words might not?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The exact percentages at a glance.' A bar graph shows the numbers clearly so you see them fast.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Ben was reading a science article that described the layers of the Earth. The article explained that our planet has a hard outer crust, a thick middle layer called the mantle, and a very hot center called the core, all fitting inside one another like the layers of an onion. Ben wanted to add one picture that would help readers clearly see how these layers are arranged from the outside to the deep inside. Which illustration would BEST help a reader understand an article about the layers of the Earth?
choices: A cross-section diagram showing the crust, mantle, and core
questions.correct: A cross-section diagram showing the crust, mantle, and core
questions.hint: You can't SEE inside the Earth, so a diagram that shows a cross-section is the best way!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A cross-section shows the layers cut open, so you can see the crust, mantle, and core in order. Great choice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, Ben wants readers to see the layers from outside to inside. Which picture shows the inside of the Earth?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'A cross-section diagram showing the crust, mantle, and core.' It shows the layers cut open, outside to inside.

questions.prompt: A book about frogs has a picture of a green frog sitting on a lily pad. Below the picture are the words: "A frog rests on a leaf in the pond." What does the picture show you?
choices: A frog sitting on a leaf in the water
A dog running in a field
A bird flying in the sky
questions.correct: A frog sitting on a leaf in the water
questions.hint: Look at what is in the picture and match it to the words below it.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The picture shows a frog on a lily pad in the pond, which is a frog on a leaf in the water. Well done!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Look at the picture again. What animal is it, and where is it sitting?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'A frog sitting on a leaf in the water.' The picture shows a green frog on a lily pad in the pond.

questions.prompt: A page about weather shows a big yellow sun with the label "Sunny Day." The text says, "On a sunny day, the sky is bright." What kind of day does the picture show?
questions.hint: The label under the picture tells you the answer in one word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A big yellow sun and a bright sky show a sunny day. Nice reading of the picture!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Look at the big yellow sun in the picture. What kind of day would that be?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'A sunny day.' The big yellow sun and bright sky in the picture show a sunny day.

questions.prompt: A science book has a diagram of a tree. One arrow points to the bottom part and is labeled "roots." What part of the tree do the roots make?
choices: The bottom part, under the ground
questions.correct: The bottom part, under the ground
questions.hint: Follow the arrow to see which part of the tree the label "roots" points to.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The arrow points to the bottom, under the ground, so that's where the roots are. Great job reading the diagram!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The arrow points to the bottom part of the tree. Where do roots grow?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The bottom part, under the ground.' The arrow points to the roots at the bottom, below the soil.

questions.prompt: A book about the sun has a photo of a garden with the caption: "Plants need sunlight to grow." What does the photo and caption help you learn?
questions.hint: The caption is the sentence right under the photo. Read it to find the answer.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The photo of a garden and its caption teach you that plants need sunlight to grow. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Read the caption under the garden photo again. What does it tell you plants need?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Plants need sunlight to grow.' The garden photo and its caption teach you that idea together.

questions.prompt: A map in a book shows a school with a red star on it. The words say, "The red star shows where the school is." What does the red star on the map show?
questions.hint: The words tell you exactly what the red star means on the map.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The words say the red star shows where the school is, so that's what the star means. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Read the words next to the map again. What does the red star point out?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Where the school is.' The words tell you the red star marks the spot of the school on the map.

standards.standard_description: Describe the logical connection between particular sentences and paragraphs in a text.
standards.parent_tip: Ask 'Why did the author put this paragraph after that one? How are they connected?'

questions.prompt:
Sentence 1: Plastic bags harm ocean animals. Sentence 2: Sea turtles mistake plastic bags for jellyfish and eat them.

Sentence 2 connects to Sentence 1 by —
choices: Giving an example of how plastic harms animals
questions.correct: Giving an example of how plastic harms animals
questions.hint: Sentence 1 makes a general point. Sentence 2 gives a specific example!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Sentence 2 tells about turtles eating bags, which is an example of how plastic harms animals. Sharp thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, Sentence 2 tells us one way turtles get hurt by plastic. How does that fit with Sentence 1?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Giving an example of how plastic harms animals.' The turtle detail is one example of the first sentence's idea.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: A rainbow is a curved band of colors that stretches across the sky, showing red, orange, yellow, green, blue, and purple in a bright arc. Rainbows appear when sunlight shines through tiny raindrops floating in the air. As the light passes into each drop, the drop bends it and splits it into all of its separate colors, and that is why we see the glowing arc after it rains. How do the first two paragraphs of the passage connect to each other?
choices: They compare two different kinds of natural events.
The first tells what something is, and the second tells why it happens.
They give a problem and then offer a solution.
The first gives an opinion, and the second gives facts to support it.
questions.correct: The first tells what something is, and the second tells why it happens.
questions.hint: Look at what information each paragraph provides about earthquakes.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The first part tells what a rainbow is, and the second tells why it happens. Great connecting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The first part describes a rainbow, and the second explains how it forms. How do those two link up?
