questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. She came from a snowstorm with chattering teeth. Which word shows the strongest, coldest feeling?
questions.reveal_feedback: The best answer is freezing. Chattering teeth and shivering show she was very, very cold, not just a little cool.

questions.prompt:
The Latin root **port** means "to carry." Read this sentence:

"The **porter** at the train station helped the travelers with their heavy suitcases."

Based on the root, what does a **porter** most likely do?
choices: Drives the train down the track
questions.hint: Focus on the root port, which means "to carry." Which job is mostly about carrying things?
questions.correct_feedback: Great thinking! Port means to carry, so a porter carries people's bags and luggage.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Remember the root port means to carry. Which job is all about carrying something heavy?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is carries people's bags and luggage. Port means to carry, and the porter helped with the heavy suitcases.

questions.prompt:
Read this sentence:

"Dad slammed the **trunk** shut after loading the cooler and the beach chairs into the back of the car."

What does **trunk** mean in this sentence?
choices: A large box for storing clothes
The thick main stem of a tree
The storage space at the back of a car
questions.correct: The storage space at the back of a car
questions.hint: Every choice is a real meaning of "trunk." Use the clues "loading the cooler" and "back of the car" to pick the meaning that fits this sentence.
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! They loaded a cooler and chairs into the back of the car, so trunk means the storage space there.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look at the sentence. Where did Dad load the cooler and chairs? That tells you which trunk this is.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is the storage space at the back of a car. The clue is loading the cooler and chairs into the back.

questions.prompt:
Read this sentence:

"The exhausted hikers ______ up the steep, muddy trail, dragging their heavy boots with every step."

Which word fills the blank most **precisely** to show how hard the climb was?
questions.hint: All four words could technically fit, but only one paints a vivid picture of slow, tired, heavy steps. Match the word to "exhausted" and "dragging."
questions.correct_feedback: Perfect! Trudged shows they walked slowly and heavily, dragging their boots. It paints the hard climb.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. The hikers were exhausted and dragging their boots. Which word shows a slow, heavy, tiring walk?
questions.reveal_feedback: The best word is trudged. It means to walk slowly and heavily, which fits the exhausted hikers dragging their boots.

questions.prompt:
Maria felt **exhausted** after running all day, so she fell asleep the moment she lay down.

What does **exhausted** mean?
questions.hint: She fell asleep right away. What do you feel when you need to sleep after a long, busy day?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! She ran all day and fell asleep right away, so exhausted means very tired.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. She ran all day and fell asleep the moment she lay down. How would that make you feel?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is very tired. Running all day and falling right asleep are the clues that exhausted means very tired.

questions.prompt:
The soup was **piping hot**, so Dad blew on his spoon to cool it before he ate.

What does **piping hot** mean?
questions.hint: Dad had to blow on his spoon to cool the soup down. Was the soup hot or cold?
questions.correct_feedback: That's right! Dad had to blow on his spoon to cool it, so piping hot means very hot.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, why would Dad blow on his spoon before eating? That clue tells you what piping hot means.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is very hot. Dad blew on his spoon to cool it down, which shows the soup was very hot.

questions.prompt:
The path was **narrow**, so only one person could walk on it at a time.

What does **narrow** mean?
questions.hint: Only one person could fit on the path at a time. Was the path wide or thin?
questions.correct_feedback: Great! Only one person could fit at a time, so narrow means not wide.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Only one person could walk on the path at a time. What does that tell you about its width?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is not wide. Since only one person fit at a time, the path was narrow, meaning not wide.

questions.prompt:
Read this sentence: "Please **turn** the page to read the next part of the story."

What does **turn** mean here?
choices: A player's go in a game
questions.hint: You do this to a page in a book to see the next one. You flip it!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! To turn the page means to flip it over so you can read the next part.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. Turn has more than one meaning. Think about what you do to a page in a book.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is to flip over. When you turn a page, you flip it over to read the next part of the story.

questions.prompt:
The old bridge was **sturdy**, so the heavy truck crossed it safely without any problem.

What does **sturdy** mean?
questions.hint: A heavy truck crossed the bridge safely. Was the bridge strong or weak?
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! The heavy truck crossed safely, so sturdy means strong.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. A heavy truck crossed the bridge safely. What does that tell you about the bridge?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is strong. A heavy truck crossed safely, so the sturdy bridge was strong.

standards.standard_description: Use sentence-level context as a clue to the meaning of a word or phrase.
standards.parent_tip: When your child hits an unknown word, say 'Read the whole sentence — what clues help you figure it out?'

questions.prompt:
The nocturnal owl hunted mice while other birds slept.

Using context clues, **nocturnal** means —
questions.hint: The owl hunted while other birds SLEPT. When do birds sleep? At night!
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! The owl hunted while other birds slept, so nocturnal means active at night.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The owl was busy hunting while the other birds slept. When was the owl active?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is active at night. The owl hunted while other birds slept, which is the clue for nocturnal.

questions.prompt:
The soup was so hot it was scalding, and it burned my tongue.

Using context clues, **scalding** means —
questions.hint: The sentence says the soup burned my tongue. What kind of hot burns you?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! It burned your tongue, so scalding means burning hot.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. The soup burned your tongue. Does that sound cold or hot to you?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is burning hot. The soup burned your tongue, which shows scalding means burning hot.

questions.prompt:
The tired hikers rested because they were exhausted after the long walk.

Using context clues, **exhausted** means —
questions.hint: The sentence says the hikers were tired and rested. What word means the same as tired?
questions.correct_feedback: That's it! The hikers rested after a long walk, so exhausted means very tired.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the hikers rested after a long walk. How do you feel after a really long walk?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is very tired. The tired hikers rested after a long walk, so exhausted means very tired.

questions.prompt:
The gigantic elephant was so big it could not fit through the door.

Using context clues, **gigantic** means —
questions.hint: The sentence says the elephant was so big it could not fit. What does that tell you about gigantic?
questions.correct_feedback: Great! It was too big to fit through the door, so gigantic means very big.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The elephant could not fit through the door. What does that tell you about its size?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is very big. The elephant was too big to fit through the door, so gigantic means very big.

questions.prompt:
Maria was thrilled and jumped for joy when she won the race.

Using context clues, **thrilled** means —
questions.hint: The sentence says Maria jumped for joy. How do you feel when you jump for joy?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! She jumped for joy after winning, so thrilled means very excited.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. Maria jumped for joy when she won. What kind of feeling makes you jump for joy?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is very excited. Jumping for joy after winning shows that thrilled means very excited.

questions.prompt:
The old bridge was fragile, so it broke easily when the truck drove over it.

Using context clues, **fragile** means —
questions.hint: The sentence says the bridge broke easily. What does fragile tell you about the bridge?
questions.correct_feedback: Right! The bridge broke easily, so fragile means easily broken.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. The old bridge broke easily when the truck drove over it. What does that tell you?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is easily broken. The bridge broke easily, so fragile means easily broken.

standards.standard_description: Determine the meaning of the new word formed when a known affix is added to a known word.
standards.parent_tip: Practice: 'If agree means to think the same, what does disagree mean? What about agreeable?'

questions.prompt: The word **impossible** means —
questions.hint: 'Im-' (like 'in-') means not. Impossible = not possible.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The prefix im- means not, so impossible means not possible.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look at the prefix im-. It means not. Add that to the word possible.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is not possible. The prefix im- means not, so impossible means not possible.

questions.prompt: After the storm knocked it down, the town had to reconstruct the old pier board by board. In this sentence, reconstruct means to —
questions.hint: Break the word into its prefix and root: re- + construct. What does 'construct' mean, and what does re- add to it?
questions.correct_feedback: Nice work! Re- means again, and they put the pier back board by board, so reconstruct means build it again.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Remember re- means again. The storm knocked the pier down and the town put it back. What were they doing?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is build it again. Re- means again plus construct means build, so reconstruct means to build it again.

questions.prompt: Grandpa's directions were so ______ that we drove in circles and got lost twice. Which word fits the blank BEST?
questions.hint: Two choices are built from 'help' but mean different things: one describes directions that don't help, the other describes a person who can't take care of themselves. Which one matches directions?
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! They drove in circles and got lost, so the directions were unhelpful. Un- means not helpful.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, they drove in circles and got lost. Were the directions good or bad? Look for the un- word that means not helpful.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is unhelpful. Un- means not, and since they got lost, the directions did not help at all.

questions.prompt: "You misjudged how far the puddle was, so you landed right in it," Mom said. Misjudged means that you judged the distance —
questions.hint: The prefix mis- shows up in 'mistake' and 'misspell.' What does mis- do to the word it joins?
questions.correct_feedback: Great! The prefix mis- means wrongly, and you landed in the puddle, so misjudged means you judged it wrongly.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look at the prefix mis-. You landed right in the puddle, so did you judge the distance right or not?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is wrongly. Mis- means wrong, so misjudged means you judged the distance wrongly and landed in the puddle.

questions.prompt: Grandma held up the old locket and whispered, "This necklace is priceless." In this sentence, priceless means the necklace is —
choices: worth so much you can't put a price on it
questions.correct: worth so much you can't put a price on it
questions.hint: The suffix -less usually means 'without.' But think about WHY something would have no price — is it because it's worthless, or because it's too valuable to price?
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! Grandma treasures the locket, so priceless means worth so much you can't put a price on it.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Grandma whispered about the special old locket with care. Does priceless mean it is worth a lot or a little?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is worth so much you can't put a price on it. Priceless means something is too valuable to measure with money.

questions.prompt: Both words are built from 'view.' Which word means to look at a movie BEFORE it is shown to everyone else?
questions.hint: One prefix means 'before' and another means 'again.' Which prefix matches 'before it is shown to everyone'?
questions.correct_feedback: Perfect! The prefix pre- means before, so preview means to look at the movie before everyone else.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Think about which prefix means before. You want the word that means seeing the movie ahead of everyone else.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is preview. Pre- means before, so a preview is a look at the movie before it is shown to everyone.

questions.prompt: The prefix **re-** means "again." So the word **replay** means to —
questions.hint: "Re-" means "again," so add that to "play."
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Re- means again, so replay means to play again.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look at the prefix re-. It means again. Add that to the word play.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is play again. Re- means again, so replay means to play again.

questions.prompt: The prefix **un-** means "not." So the word **unhappy** means —
questions.hint: "Un-" means "not," so put that in front of "happy."
questions.correct_feedback: Great! Un- means not, so unhappy means not happy.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Remember the prefix un- means not. Put that together with the word happy.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is not happy. Un- means not, so unhappy means not happy.

questions.prompt: The suffix **-ful** means "full of." So the word **helpful** means —
questions.hint: "-ful" means "full of," so it is full of help.
questions.correct_feedback: That's it! The suffix -ful means full of, so helpful means full of help.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look at the suffix -ful. It means full of. Add that to the word help.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is full of help. The suffix -ful means full of, so helpful means full of help.

questions.prompt: The prefix **un-** means "not." So the word **unlock** means to —
questions.hint: "Un-" means "not," so "unlock" is the opposite of lock.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Un- means not, so unlock means to not lock, or to open it.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Remember un- means not. What is the opposite of locking something up?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is not lock, or to open it. Un- means not, so unlock means to open what was locked.

questions.prompt: The suffix **-less** means "without." So the word **fearless** means —
questions.hint: "-less" means "without," so it means without fear.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! The suffix -less means without, so fearless means without fear.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look at the suffix -less. It means without. Add that to the word fear.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is without fear. The suffix -less means without, so fearless means without fear.

standards.standard_description: Use a known root word as a clue to the meaning of an unknown word with the same root.
standards.parent_tip: Show your child how 'sign' connects to 'signal,' 'signature,' and 'design.'

questions.prompt: You know **graph** means "to write or draw." Which word is related to writing or drawing?
questions.hint: Photo = light. Graph = write/draw. A photograph is written with light!
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! Graph means to write or draw, and a photograph is a drawn or captured picture. Same root!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Look for the word that has graph inside it. Graph means to write or draw, so which word is about pictures?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is photograph. It has the root graph, which means to write or draw, and a photograph captures a picture.

questions.prompt: You know the root **port** means "to carry." Read this sentence: "The campers brought a portable stove they could carry from site to site." Using the root, what does **portable** most likely mean?
questions.hint: The sentence talks about cooking, but the ROOT tells you the real meaning. Find the part of the word that means "to carry."
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Port means to carry, and the campers could carry the stove, so portable means able to be carried.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Remember the root port means to carry. The campers moved the stove from site to site. What does portable mean?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is able to be carried. Port means to carry, so a portable stove is one you can carry along.

questions.prompt: The root **aud** means "to hear." Read: "Hundreds of people packed the auditorium for the concert." Using the root, an **auditorium** is a place built mainly for people to —
questions.hint: Ignore what a big room COULD be used for. Find the root "aud" and let its meaning point you to the answer.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! Aud means to hear, and people packed in for a concert, so an auditorium is a place to hear a performance.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look at the root aud, which means to hear. Why did the crowd pack the auditorium for a concert?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is hear a performance. Aud means to hear, so an auditorium is built for people to hear a show.

questions.prompt: The root **dict** means "to speak or say." Which word is built from this root and names a person who tells others what to do by giving spoken commands?
questions.hint: First check which words actually CONTAIN the root "dict." A word can sound bossy but not have the root at all.
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! Dict means to speak, so a dictator is someone who gives spoken commands to others.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Look for the word built from dict, which means to speak. Which one names a person who gives spoken commands?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is dictator. It comes from dict, meaning to speak, and a dictator tells others what to do with commands.

questions.prompt: You know **struct** means "to build." Read: "The instructions showed us how to put the bookshelf together." The word **instructions** shares this root, so instructions most likely means information that helps you —
choices: find your way to a place
questions.hint: Locate the root "struct" inside the word and match its "build" meaning to the choices.
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! Struct means to build, so instructions help you build or put something together, like the bookshelf.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Remember the root struct means to build. The instructions showed how to put a bookshelf together. What do they help you do?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is build or put something together. Struct means to build, so instructions help you build the bookshelf.

questions.prompt: The root **spect** means "to look or watch." Read: "Grandpa put on his spectacles so he could read the tiny print." Using the root and the sentence, **spectacles** are —
questions.hint: The root "spect" is about the eyes, not the ears. Match it to the reading clue in the sentence.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Spect means to look, and Grandpa read tiny print with them, so spectacles are glasses you look through.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look at the root spect, which means to look. Grandpa used spectacles to read tiny print. What are they?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is glasses you look through. Spect means to look, so spectacles are glasses that help you see.

questions.prompt: You know that **bake** means "to cook in an oven." Which word uses the root **bake**?
questions.hint: Look for the word that has "bake" hiding inside it.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! Baker has the root bake inside it, plus -er for the person who bakes.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Look for the word that has bake hiding inside it. Which one is about cooking in an oven?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is baker. It uses the root bake, and a baker is a person who bakes.

questions.prompt: You know that **play** means "to have fun with a game." Which word uses the root **play**?
questions.hint: Find the word that begins with "play."
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Playful has the root play inside it, meaning full of play.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Look for the word that has play hiding inside it. Which one is about having fun?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is playful. It uses the root play, and playful means full of play.

questions.prompt: You know that **help** means "to give aid." Which word uses the root **help**?
questions.hint: Which word do you see the word "help" inside of?
questions.correct_feedback: Great! Helper has the root help inside it, meaning a person who helps.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Look for the word that has help hiding inside it. Which one is about giving aid?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is helper. It uses the root help, and a helper is a person who gives aid.

questions.prompt: You know that **farm** means "land where crops grow." Which word uses the root **farm**?
questions.hint: Look for "farm" at the start of the word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Farmer has the root farm inside it, meaning a person who works on a farm.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Look for the word that has farm hiding inside it. Which one is about land where crops grow?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is farmer. It uses the root farm, and a farmer works on a farm.

questions.prompt: You know that **paint** means "to put color on something." Which word uses the root **paint**?
questions.hint: Find the word that has "paint" inside it.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! Painting has the root paint inside it, meaning something made by painting.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Look for the word that has paint hiding inside it. Which one is about putting color on something?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is painting. It uses the root paint, and a painting is made by painting.

standards.standard_description: Use glossaries or beginning dictionaries to determine or clarify the precise meaning of key words.
standards.parent_tip: Practice dictionary skills — guide words, alphabetical order, multiple definitions.

questions.prompt:
A dictionary entry for **bark** shows two definitions: 1. The outer covering of a tree. 2. The sound a dog makes. How do you know which definition fits?

"The bark of the old oak tree was rough and gray."
questions.hint: It's describing a tree, so it's the outer covering — definition 1!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The sentence talks about a tree, so definition 1, the outer covering of a tree, fits best.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Read the sentence again. Is it about a dog or a tree? Match that clue to the right definition.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is definition 1. The sentence is about an oak tree, so bark means the outer covering of a tree.

questions.prompt:
A dictionary entry for **current** lists these meanings:
1. happening right now
2. a steady flow of water or air
3. a flow of electric charge
4. the newest or most up-to-date thing

Sentence: "The lifeguard warned us that a strong current was pulling swimmers away from the shore." Which meaning fits this sentence?
choices: A steady flow of water or air
The newest or most up-to-date thing
questions.correct: A steady flow of water or air
questions.hint: Ignore the meaning of the word you hear most often. Reread the whole sentence and ask what is actually pulling the swimmers.
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! The current was pulling swimmers in the water, so it means a steady flow of water or air.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Read the sentence again. The current was in the ocean pulling swimmers. Which meaning matches water?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is a steady flow of water or air. The current pulled swimmers at the shore, so it is the water meaning.

questions.prompt:
A dictionary entry for **wind** shows two pronunciations:
- **wind** (rhymes with 'pinned'): moving air outdoors
- **wind** (rhymes with 'find'): to turn or tighten something by twisting it

Sentence: "Grandpa has to wind his old watch every morning so it keeps the right time." Which meaning fits?
choices: To lose your breath after running
To turn or tighten something by twisting it
To follow a curving path, the way a river bends
questions.correct: To turn or tighten something by twisting it
questions.hint: Picture exactly what Grandpa does to the watch with his fingers, then match that action to a definition.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! Grandpa turns his watch to keep it running, so wind means to turn or tighten by twisting.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Read the sentence again. Grandpa does something to his watch each morning to keep it going. Which meaning fits?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is to turn or tighten something by twisting it. Grandpa winds his watch by twisting it each morning.

questions.prompt: A dictionary page has the guide words **trap** and **tray** printed at the top. Which one of these words would you find on that page?
questions.hint: Guide words show the first and last words on the page. Compare each choice letter by letter until you find one that lands between them.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Guide words trap and tray come in ABC order, and travel fits right between them.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Use ABC order. The guide words are trap and tray. Which word would land between those two alphabetically?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is travel. In ABC order it falls between the guide words trap and tray, so it is on that page.

questions.prompt:
A science glossary gives these entries:
- **transparent**: clear; light passes straight through so you can see objects on the other side
- **translucent**: lets some light through, but you cannot see clear shapes
- **opaque**: lets no light through at all

Sentence: "The frosted glass in the front door is ___: sunlight glows softly through it, but you cannot tell who is standing outside." Which word belongs in the blank?
questions.hint: Read the glossary carefully and match it to the two clues: light DOES get through, but you CANNOT see shapes.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! Light glows through but you can't see clear shapes, so the glass is translucent.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Read the clue again. Some light glows through, but you cannot see clear shapes. Which word matches that in-between?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is translucent. It means some light passes through but you cannot see clear shapes, just like the frosted glass.

questions.prompt:
A dictionary entry for **point** lists these meanings:
1. the sharp end of something
2. the main idea a person is trying to make
3. a unit used to keep score in a game
4. a particular moment or spot in time

Sentence: "With three seconds left on the clock, Maya sank the basket that gave her team the winning point." Which meaning fits?
choices: A unit used to keep score in a game
The main idea a person is trying to make
A particular moment or spot in time
questions.correct: A unit used to keep score in a game
questions.hint: The sentence mentions a clock and a last-second moment, but focus on what 'winning point' means in a basketball game.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Maya's basket won the game by scoring, so point means a unit used to keep score.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Read the sentence again. Maya's basket helped her team win the game. Which meaning of point fits a game?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is a unit used to keep score in a game. Maya's winning basket scored the deciding point.

questions.prompt: A dictionary shows: **puppy** (noun): a young dog. What does the word "puppy" mean?
questions.hint: Read the dictionary meaning right after the word. It tells you exactly what "puppy" means.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The dictionary told us puppy means a young dog, and you used it perfectly. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, let's look at the dictionary again. It tells you exactly what the word means. Read that little definition one more time.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'A young dog.' The dictionary said puppy means a young dog, so that's the one to pick.

questions.prompt: A dictionary shows: **damp** (adjective): a little bit wet. If a towel is damp, what is it?
questions.hint: The dictionary already gives the meaning. "Damp" means a little bit wet.
questions.correct_feedback: That's right! The dictionary said damp means a little bit wet, so a damp towel is a little bit wet. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The dictionary gives you the meaning of damp. Peek back at those words and match them to a choice.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'A little bit wet.' The dictionary told us damp means a little bit wet, so that's what a damp towel is.

questions.prompt: A dictionary shows: **gigantic** (adjective): very, very big. Which word means the same as "gigantic"?
questions.hint: The dictionary says "gigantic" means very, very big. Which choice also means very big?
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! Gigantic means very, very big, and huge means the same thing. Super job matching!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Think about what gigantic means. Which word feels really, really big like that?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Huge.' Gigantic means very big, and huge also means very big, so they match.

questions.prompt: A glossary in a science book shows: **stem** (noun): the part of a plant that holds up the leaves and flowers. What does the stem do?
choices: Holds up the leaves and flowers
questions.correct: Holds up the leaves and flowers
questions.hint: The glossary meaning is written right there. It tells you what the stem does.
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! The glossary told us the stem holds up the leaves and flowers. You read it carefully!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The glossary in the science book explains the stem's job. Read that sentence again slowly.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Holds up the leaves and flowers.' The glossary said that is exactly what the stem does.

questions.prompt: A dictionary shows: **whisper** (verb): to talk in a very soft, quiet voice. If you whisper, how are you talking?
questions.hint: Look at the dictionary meaning. To "whisper" is to talk soft and quiet.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Whisper means to talk in a soft, quiet voice. You matched the meaning perfectly!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Think about how you sound when you whisper a secret. Loud or soft? Read the definition again.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'In a soft, quiet voice.' The dictionary said whisper means to talk very softly and quietly.

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate understanding of figurative language, word relationships, and nuances in word meanings.
standards.parent_tip: Point out figurative language in everyday life: 'It's raining cats and dogs!' means it's raining very hard.

questions.prompt:
She had a heart of gold.

This means —
choices: She was very kind and generous
Her heart was made of gold metal
questions.correct: She was very kind and generous
questions.hint: This is figurative language — a 'heart of gold' means a person who is very kind.
questions.correct_feedback: Wonderful! A heart of gold doesn't mean real gold. It means a person is very kind and generous, just like you said!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! A heart of gold isn't about real metal or money. It describes what kind of person she is inside.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'She was very kind and generous.' A heart of gold is a way to say someone is really kind.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses a simile?
choices: She was as fast as a rocket.
questions.correct: She was as fast as a rocket.
questions.hint: A simile compares two things using 'as' or 'like.'
questions.correct_feedback: Great eye! A simile compares two things using 'like' or 'as,' and 'as fast as a rocket' does just that. Nice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! A simile compares two things using the words 'like' or 'as.' Which sentence has one of those little words?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'She was as fast as a rocket.' It uses the word 'as' to compare, so it's a simile.

questions.prompt: What does **break a leg** mean?
questions.hint: This is an idiom — it means the opposite of what it sounds like! Performers say it for luck.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Break a leg is a fun way people say good luck, especially before a show. You knew it!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Break a leg is a saying, so it doesn't really mean hurting your leg. What do people wish before a big show?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Good luck.' Break a leg is a friendly way to wish someone good luck before they perform.

questions.prompt: Which two words are antonyms (opposites)?
questions.hint: Antonyms are opposites. Which pair shows opposite meanings?
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! Antonyms are opposites, and hot and cold are opposites. Well done!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, antonyms are opposite words. The other pairs mean almost the same thing. Which pair is truly opposite?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Hot and cold.' Those two words are opposites, and opposites are called antonyms.

questions.prompt:
The thunder grumbled like an angry old man.

This is an example of —
questions.hint: Thunder 'grumbling' is personification (human action). 'Like an angry old man' is a simile!
questions.correct_feedback: Impressive! Thunder can't really grumble, so that's personification, and 'like an angry old man' is a simile. You caught both!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Thunder is given a human action, and it's compared using 'like.' Think about which two kinds of language that shows.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Personification and simile.' Thunder grumbling acts human, and 'like an angry old man' compares with 'like.'

standards.standard_description: Distinguish the literal and nonliteral meanings of words and phrases in context.
standards.parent_tip: Ask 'Does this sentence mean exactly what it says, or is it saying something in a creative way?'

questions.prompt:
Time flies when you're having fun.

Is this literal or nonliteral?
choices: Nonliteral — it means time seems to pass quickly
questions.correct: Nonliteral — it means time seems to pass quickly
questions.hint: Time doesn't actually fly! It's a figurative way of saying time SEEMS to go fast.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Time doesn't really have wings. It just feels quick when you're having fun. That's nonliteral!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Does time really grow wings and fly? Think about what the saying is really telling you.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Nonliteral, it means time seems to pass quickly.' Time can't really fly, so the words don't mean it exactly.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is LITERAL (means exactly what it says)?
choices: The cat sat on the mat.
She has butterflies in her stomach.
questions.correct: The cat sat on the mat.
questions.hint: Only one of these sentences describes something that could actually, physically happen.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! A cat sitting on a mat means exactly what it says. That's literal language. Nice thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Literal means the words mean exactly what they say. Some of these are sayings that mean something else. Which one is real?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The cat sat on the mat.' It means exactly what it says, so it is literal.

questions.prompt:
My backpack weighs a ton.

What does this really mean?
choices: The backpack is made of metal
questions.hint: A backpack can't really weigh a ton! This is an exaggeration (hyperbole).
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! A backpack doesn't really weigh a ton. It just feels super heavy. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! A backpack can't really weigh 2,000 pounds. It's a saying. What is it really telling you about the backpack?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The backpack feels very heavy.' Weighs a ton is a fun way to say something feels really heavy.

questions.prompt:
She was drowning in homework.

This means —
choices: She fell into a pool of papers
She had way too much homework
questions.correct: She had way too much homework
questions.hint: You can't literally drown in homework! It means she had an overwhelming amount.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! She wasn't really in water. Drowning in homework means she had way too much of it. Smart!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! There's no real water here. Drowning in homework is a saying. Think about how much homework she had.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'She had way too much homework.' Drowning in it means having so much it feels overwhelming.

questions.prompt:
His words cut like a knife.

This sentence means his words were —
questions.hint: Words can't actually cut, but they CAN hurt feelings — like a knife hurts physically.
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! Words aren't really knives. Cut like a knife means the words were sharp and hurtful. Well done!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The words aren't a real knife. Think about how a knife feels sharp, and how that describes his words.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Sharp and hurtful.' Words that cut like a knife hurt someone's feelings.

questions.prompt:
When Dad heard the bad news, his heart sank.

What does "his heart sank" MOST precisely mean here?
choices: He suddenly felt very sad and discouraged
He became very angry and shouted
He felt just a little worried
He had a problem with his heart
questions.correct: He suddenly felt very sad and discouraged
questions.hint: The phrase isn't about his real heart. Match how STRONG the feeling is to 'bad news' — not too mild, not the wrong feeling.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! His heart didn't really move. It's a way to say he suddenly felt very sad and discouraged by the bad news. Great!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! His heart didn't really sink. Remember, he just heard bad news. How would that make him feel?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'He suddenly felt very sad and discouraged.' Heart sank shows sudden sadness after bad news.

questions.prompt: In which sentence is "hit the roof" used NONLITERALLY (not meaning exactly what the words say)?
choices: Rain hit the roof loudly all night long.
When she saw the huge mess, Mom hit the roof.
The baseball flew up and hit the roof of the shed.
The workers hit the roof with their hammers to fix it.
questions.correct: When she saw the huge mess, Mom hit the roof.
questions.hint: Three sentences describe something really touching a roof. One uses the phrase to describe a feeling instead.
questions.correct_feedback: Great thinking! When Mom hit the roof, she got very angry. That's nonliteral, since she didn't touch a real roof. Nice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Nonliteral means the words don't mean exactly what they say. In three sentences something really touches a roof. Which one doesn't?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'When she saw the huge mess, Mom hit the roof.' Here it means she got very angry, not a real roof.

questions.prompt:
Grandpa called the new no-phones-at-dinner rule "a blessing in disguise." At first everyone complained, but soon the family talked and laughed more at dinner.

What does "a blessing in disguise" mean?
choices: A rule that nobody in the family likes
A special costume worn to a party
Something that seems bad at first but turns out to be good
A gift that is wrapped up so you can't see it
questions.correct: Something that seems bad at first but turns out to be good
questions.hint: Use the story clue: it started with complaints but ended with something good. Don't get stuck on the everyday meanings of 'blessing' or 'disguise.'
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The rule seemed bad at first, but it turned out good. That's a blessing in disguise. Wonderful reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! It's not a costume or a wrapped gift. Remember, everyone complained but then liked it. Think about that change.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Something that seems bad at first but turns out to be good,' just like the dinner rule did.

questions.prompt: Which phrase means the SAME as "let the cat out of the bag"?
questions.hint: Think about what really happens when a secret 'gets out.' Two choices sound close because telling a secret can feel like a mistake or like setting something loose.
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! Let the cat out of the bag means you accidentally told a secret. There's no real cat! Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! There's no real cat or bag here. It's a saying about a secret. Think about what happens when a secret slips out.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Accidentally told a secret.' Letting the cat out of the bag means a secret got shared by mistake.

questions.prompt:
The coach told the team, "Keep your eyes peeled for a good pass."

What does "keep your eyes peeled" mean?
choices: Close your eyes and rest them
Squint to see something far away
questions.hint: A coach wants players paying attention during a game. The word 'peeled' is used in a figurative way, not the way you peel fruit.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Keep your eyes peeled means to watch very carefully. You didn't get tricked by the word peeled. Nice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! You don't really peel your eyes! Think about what the coach wants the team to do with their eyes.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Watch very carefully.' Keep your eyes peeled means to look closely for something.

standards.standard_description: Identify real-life connections between words and their use.
standards.parent_tip: Connect vocabulary to real experiences: 'When have YOU felt courageous? What made it brave?'

questions.prompt: When would you describe someone as **generous**?
choices: When they keep everything for themselves
When they share with others willingly
questions.correct: When they share with others willingly
questions.hint: Generous means giving freely. Think of someone who shares!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A generous person happily shares with others. You knew just what that word means. Great!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, generous is about how someone treats others. It's not about height or speed. Which choice shows sharing?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'When they share with others willingly.' That giving, sharing spirit is what generous means.

questions.prompt: Which situation shows **cooperation**?
choices: A student working alone on a test
A group building a fort together
questions.correct: A group building a fort together
questions.hint: Cooperation means working together. Which choice shows teamwork?
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! Cooperation means working together, and a group building a fort together does just that. Well done!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Cooperation means people working together. Which choice shows more than one person teaming up?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'A group building a fort together.' Working as a team like that is cooperation.

questions.prompt: Which word BEST describes how you feel when you try something new and scary?
questions.hint: Trying something new and scary can make you feel nervous or anxious.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! When you try something new and scary, you feel anxious, or nervous inside. You matched it well!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Think about how your tummy feels before something new and scary. Would you feel calm or bored?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Anxious.' That worried, jittery feeling when something is new and scary is called anxious.

questions.prompt: A person who always tells the truth can be described as —
questions.hint: Someone truthful is honest — a word connected to trust.
questions.correct_feedback: That's right! A person who always tells the truth is honest. Great vocabulary work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Think about a word that means always telling the truth. Which choice fits a truthful person?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Honest.' Someone who always tells the truth is called honest.

questions.prompt: Which word describes a person who keeps trying even when things are difficult?
questions.hint: Think of someone who doesn't give up. They per-sist!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Persistent means you keep trying even when things get hard. You never gave up, and neither would they!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Think about a word for someone who keeps going even when it's tough. Which choice sounds like never giving up?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Persistent.' Someone who keeps trying even when things are difficult is persistent.

questions.prompt: When would you call someone **kind**?
choices: When they help a friend who is hurt
When they are taking a nap
When they are wearing a blue shirt
questions.correct: When they help a friend who is hurt
questions.hint: Think about what a kind person does for others. Which one is a nice, caring thing to do?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Helping a hurt friend is a kind thing to do. You understand what kind means!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, kind is about being nice and caring to others. Napping or wearing a shirt isn't kind or unkind. Which choice helps someone?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'When they help a friend who is hurt.' Helping a hurt friend is a kind thing to do.

questions.prompt: Which of these is something **brave**?
choices: Speaking up in front of the whole class
questions.correct: Speaking up in front of the whole class
questions.hint: Brave means doing something even when it feels scary. Which choice takes courage?
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! Speaking up in front of the whole class takes courage. That's brave! Nice job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Brave means doing something even when it feels scary. Which choice would take real courage?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Speaking up in front of the whole class.' That takes courage, which is what brave means.

questions.prompt: When would you say a person is **hungry**?
choices: When they turn on a lamp
When their stomach is growling and they want food
questions.correct: When their stomach is growling and they want food
questions.hint: Hungry is how you feel when your body needs food. Which choice is about wanting to eat?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! When your stomach growls and you want food, you feel hungry. You knew it!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Hungry is about your body wanting food. Which choice sounds like your tummy needs something to eat?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'When their stomach is growling and they want food.' That empty, growly feeling means hungry.

questions.prompt: Which one shows a **messy** room?
choices: A room where everything is put away neatly
A room with an empty desk
Toys and clothes all over the floor
questions.correct: Toys and clothes all over the floor
questions.hint: Messy means not clean or not put away. Which room looks like a big jumble?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Toys and clothes all over the floor makes a messy room. Great job picturing it!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Messy means things are not put away and are all over the place. Which room sounds cluttered, not neat?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Toys and clothes all over the floor.' Things scattered everywhere makes a room messy.

questions.prompt: When would you feel **tired**?
choices: After staying up all night long
questions.correct: After staying up all night long
questions.hint: Tired is how you feel when you need to rest or sleep. Which choice would wear you out?
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! Staying up all night long would make anyone tired. You matched the feeling perfectly!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Tired means your body needs rest and sleep. Which choice would use up your energy and wear you out?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'After staying up all night long.' Missing sleep like that makes you feel tired.

standards.standard_description: Distinguish shades of meaning among related words that describe states of mind or degrees of certainty.
standards.parent_tip: Play 'word ladders' — arrange words by intensity: annoyed → angry → furious.

questions.prompt: Which word shows the STRONGEST feeling of anger?
questions.hint: All these words relate to anger, but which one is the most intense?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Furious is the strongest way to say angry, even stronger than annoyed or bothered. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Some anger words are mild, like bothered. You want the biggest, strongest anger word here.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Furious.' Furious means super, super angry, the strongest of these words.

questions.prompt: Which word means MORE than **happy** but LESS than **ecstatic**?
questions.hint: Happy → delighted → ecstatic. Delighted is in the middle!
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! Delighted is more than happy but not as huge as ecstatic. You placed it just right!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, you need a happy word that fits between happy and ecstatic. Sad and okay aren't between them. Which one is a strong happy?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Delighted.' Delighted is more than happy but not quite as big as ecstatic.

questions.prompt:
I heard the rumor about the snow day." vs. "I knew school would be canceled.

Which shows MORE certainty?
questions.hint: Hearing something is secondhand information. Knowing is being sure!
questions.correct_feedback: Great thinking! 'I knew' shows the person was sure, while 'I heard' is just a rumor. You spotted the certainty!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! One sentence just heard a rumor, and one felt sure. Which word means the person really knew for certain?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that 'I knew' shows more certainty. Knowing something is much more sure than just hearing a rumor.

questions.prompt: Read the sentences. Which one shows that Dad is LEAST sure it will rain?
questions.hint: Line the words up from most sure to least sure: 'definitely/sure' are top, then 'probably,' then the weakest one.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Might' shows Dad is the least sure it will rain. The other words sound much more certain. Great!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Some words like 'definitely' sound very sure. You want the one that sounds the least sure, like a maybe.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'It might rain later.' The word 'might' shows he's the least sure it will happen.

questions.prompt: Which word shows MORE certainty than **maybe** but LESS certainty than **definitely**?
questions.hint: Two of these mean almost the same as 'maybe' or 'definitely' — you need the one that sits in the middle of the ladder.
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! Probably is more sure than maybe but not as sure as definitely. You placed it perfectly!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, you need a word between maybe and definitely. 'Never' and 'possibly' don't fit there. Which shows pretty sure, but not totally?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Probably.' Probably is more sure than maybe but less sure than definitely.

questions.prompt: Which word shows the STRONGEST feeling of fear?
questions.hint: Picture the feelings from a tiny worry up to complete panic, and pick the one at the very top.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Terrified is the strongest fear word, much bigger than nervous or uneasy. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Some fear words are mild, like nervous or uneasy. You want the biggest, strongest fear word here.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Terrified.' Terrified means extremely scared, the strongest fear of these words.

questions.prompt: Which word describes the DEEPEST sadness?
questions.hint: Rank them from a little bit sad to totally sad, then choose the heaviest one.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Miserable is the deepest, heaviest sadness, more than gloomy or disappointed. You knew it!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Some words are only a little sad, like down or disappointed. You want the deepest, saddest word.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Miserable.' Miserable means the deepest, heaviest kind of sadness.

questions.prompt: Maria said, "I'm POSITIVE the library is open today." In this sentence, **positive** means —
questions.hint: 'Positive' has several meanings — use the rest of Maria's sentence to decide which one fits here.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! When Maria says she's positive, she means she's certain, or totally sure. You caught the right meaning!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Positive has more than one meaning. Here Maria is telling how sure she is. Which choice matches being sure?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Certain.' When Maria says she's positive, she means she is certain, or totally sure.

questions.prompt: Which word means a BIGGER feeling of happy than just "glad"?
questions.hint: Look for the word that is still about being happy, only stronger. Three of the choices are not about happy at all.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Overjoyed is an even bigger happy feeling than glad. You picked the strong one!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, you need a bigger happy word than glad. Hungry, cold, and tired aren't happy at all. Which one is a huge happy?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Overjoyed.' Overjoyed means a much bigger feeling of happy than glad.

questions.prompt: Which word shows the STRONGEST fear?
questions.hint: Only one choice is a fear word. Pick the one that means very, very scared.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Terrified is the strongest fear word. The others aren't about being scared at all. Great!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Sleepy, bored, and curious aren't fear words. Which one means really, really scared?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Terrified.' Terrified means extremely scared, the strongest fear here.

questions.prompt: Sam was SURE he locked the door. Which word means Sam felt SURE?
questions.hint: "Sure" and "certain" mean the same thing. The other words do not tell how sure you feel.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Sam felt sure, and certain means sure. You matched the meaning well!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! You want a word that means sure. Slow, loud, and silly aren't about being sure. Which one fits?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Certain.' Certain means sure, which is how Sam felt about locking the door.

questions.prompt: Which word shows the STRONGEST anger?
questions.hint: Look for the anger word. Furious means way past just a little mad.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Furious is the strongest anger word. The others aren't about anger at all. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, fast, funny, and friendly aren't angry words. Which one means really, really angry?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Furious.' Furious means very, very angry, the strongest anger word here.

questions.prompt: Maya was NOT sure it would rain. Which word means Maya felt NOT sure?
questions.hint: "Not sure" is another way to say "unsure." The other words do not tell how sure she felt.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Maya felt not sure, and unsure means not sure. You matched it perfectly!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! You want a word meaning not sure. Brave, quiet, and happy don't fit. Which one means not sure?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Unsure.' Unsure means not sure, which is how Maya felt about the rain.

standards.standard_description: Acquire and use accurately conversational, general academic, and domain-specific words and phrases.
standards.parent_tip: Encourage your child to use precise words: instead of 'nice,' try 'generous,' 'thoughtful,' or 'delightful.'

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses more precise vocabulary?
choices: The pasta was delicious and perfectly seasoned.
questions.correct: The pasta was delicious and perfectly seasoned.
questions.hint: Precise words paint a clearer picture. Which sentence tells you the MOST?
questions.correct_feedback: Great! Delicious and perfectly seasoned paints a clear picture. That's precise, colorful vocabulary. Nice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Words like 'stuff,' 'okay,' and 'good' are pretty plain. Which sentence uses rich, exact describing words?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The pasta was delicious and perfectly seasoned.' Those exact words paint a clearer picture.

questions.prompt: In science, **habitat** means —
choices: The natural home of an animal or plant
questions.correct: The natural home of an animal or plant
questions.hint: This is a domain-specific word used in science to describe where organisms live.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! In science, a habitat is the natural home of an animal or plant. You knew the science word!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, habitat is a science word about where living things live. Think about where an animal or plant makes its home.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The natural home of an animal or plant.' A habitat is where a living thing naturally lives.

questions.prompt: Pick the best word to replace 'said' in this sentence.
questions.hint: She can't believe it! That shows surprise and excitement.

questions.prompt: The academic word **compare** means to —
choices: Find how things are alike and different
questions.correct: Find how things are alike and different
questions.hint: 'Compare' is a word you use in all subjects — it means looking at similarities and differences.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! To compare means to look at two things and see how they are alike and how they are different. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about what you do when you hold two things side by side. What are you really checking for?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'find how things are alike and different.' When you compare, you look at two things and notice what matches and what doesn't.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "The tired dog wanted to rest." What does the word **tired** mean?
questions.hint: The sentence says the dog wanted to rest. What kind of dog wants to rest?
questions.correct_feedback: That's right! A tired dog wants to rest because being tired means it needs sleep. You used the sentence clues well!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The dog wanted to rest. What kind of feeling makes you want to lie down and stop?
questions.reveal_feedback: The word tired means 'needing sleep or rest.' That's why the dog wanted to rest in the sentence.

questions.prompt: In science class, an animal's **home** is the place where it lives. Which word means the same as **home**?
questions.hint: A home is a place. What does the sentence tell you a home is?
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! A home is the place where an animal lives, just like your home is where you live. Nice job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The clue says a home is a place. Which choice tells about a place, not food or fur?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'where it lives.' A home means the place an animal lives, just like the sentence told us.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses a more exact, describing word?
choices: The juicy, red apple looked sweet.
questions.correct: The juicy, red apple looked sweet.
questions.hint: Which sentence gives you clear details you can picture, like color and taste?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'The juicy, red apple looked sweet' uses exact words that help you picture it. Describing words make writing come alive!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Words like 'stuff' and 'thing' are fuzzy. Which sentence paints a clear picture in your mind?
questions.reveal_feedback: The best one is 'The juicy, red apple looked sweet.' It uses exact, describing words so you can really picture the apple.

questions.prompt: Read: "The huge elephant was the biggest animal at the zoo." What does **huge** mean?
questions.hint: The sentence says the elephant was the biggest animal. So huge means very...?
questions.correct_feedback: Right! Huge means very big, and the elephant was the biggest animal at the zoo. You used the sentence clue perfectly!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The sentence says the elephant was the biggest animal there. So what does huge tell us about its size?
questions.reveal_feedback: Huge means 'very big.' The sentence even said the elephant was the biggest animal at the zoo.

questions.prompt: In a story, someone who feels **happy** feels —
questions.hint: Think about how you feel when something good happens. That is what happy means.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Someone who feels happy feels glad and cheerful. You matched the feeling just right!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Think about how your face feels when something wonderful happens. Which words match a happy feeling?
questions.reveal_feedback: Happy means 'glad and cheerful.' Those words describe the good, sunny feeling of being happy.

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate command of the conventions of standard English grammar and usage when writing or speaking
standards.parent_tip: Verb tenses, subject-verb agreement, comparatives.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a verb to show something happened in the past?
choices: My little brother run fast at the race.
My little brother running fast at the race.
My little brother ran fast at the race.
My little brother runs fast at the race.
questions.correct: My little brother ran fast at the race.
questions.hint: Remember that some action words change completely when you talk about something that already happened.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! 'Ran' is the past-tense of run, so it tells us the race already happened. You picked the right verb!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! You need a verb that shows the race already finished. Say each one aloud and listen for past-tense.
questions.reveal_feedback: The correct sentence is 'My little brother ran fast at the race.' 'Ran' is the past-tense of run, so it shows it already happened.

questions.prompt: Which sentence agrees correctly?
questions.hint: Dogs is plural — use run, not runs.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'The dogs run fast' matches. More than one dog needs the verb 'run,' so they agree. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Check if the naming word is one or many, then see if the verb matches it. Read each one softly.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The dogs run fast.' Since there is more than one dog, the verb 'run' matches them correctly.

questions.prompt: What is the comparative form of **fast**?
questions.hint: Add -er to compare two things!
questions.correct_feedback: Right! 'Faster' compares two things, like one runner is faster than another. You know your comparing words!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, a comparative word compares just two things. Which word adds -er to fast to do that?
questions.reveal_feedback: The comparative form is 'faster.' We add -er to fast when we compare two things.

questions.prompt: Which is the superlative form of **happy**?
questions.hint: Use -est when comparing three or more.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Happiest' is superlative, meaning the most happy of all. You spotted the top-of-the-list word!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! A superlative shows the most of something out of all. Which word means the MOST happy?
questions.reveal_feedback: The superlative form is 'happiest.' It means the most happy of everyone, more than happier.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the right verb tense?
choices: I am eating an apple now.
I were eating an apple now.
I am eat an apple now.
I am ate an apple now.
questions.correct: I am eating an apple now.
questions.hint: Now means present. Use am eating!
questions.correct_feedback: Great! 'I am eating an apple now' is right. 'Am eating' shows it is happening right now. Perfect tense!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The word 'now' means it is happening this moment. Which verb sounds right for right now?
questions.reveal_feedback: The correct sentence is 'I am eating an apple now.' 'Am eating' shows the action is happening right now.

questions.prompt: Ben ran faster than Sara. Tom ran faster than Ben. Which sentence is correct?
questions.hint: Line up who beat whom to find the single fastest runner, then pick the form used when comparing three or more.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Tom beat Ben, who beat Sara, so Tom is the fastest of all three. You compared them carefully!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Line them up: Sara, then Ben beat her, then Tom beat Ben. Who ends up on top?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Tom ran the fastest.' Tom beat Ben, and Ben beat Sara, so Tom is fastest of all three.

questions.prompt: Choose the sentence that uses the correct past-tense verbs: 'Yesterday our class ___ to the museum and ___ many old paintings.'
questions.hint: Both blanks need the plain past-tense form that stands alone without a helper word like 'has' or 'had.'
questions.correct_feedback: Right! 'Went, saw' are both correct past-tense verbs. The class already went and already saw the paintings. Nice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Both blanks happened yesterday, so both verbs need past-tense. Say the pairs aloud and listen carefully.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'went, saw.' Both are the correct past-tense forms, since the trip happened yesterday.

questions.prompt: Which word best completes the sentence to show WHY it happened? '___ she practiced every day, Mia became a wonderful violin player.'
questions.hint: You need a joining word that can start the sentence and signals a reason or cause.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Because' tells us WHY Mia got so good. It connects her practice to her success. Great choice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the question asks which word shows the reason WHY. Which one answers 'why did this happen?'
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Because.' It shows the reason why Mia became great, since she practiced every day.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct word: 'Of everyone in the room, Jonah spoke ___, so no one could hear him at all.'
questions.hint: You are comparing Jonah to a whole group, and the word describes HOW he spoke, so it needs the -ly adverb form.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! 'Most quietly' means the quietest of everyone, which is why no one could hear him. Well done!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Jonah was quieter than everyone in the room. Which word shows the MOST out of the whole group?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'most quietly.' Since Jonah was quieter than everyone, we use 'most' to compare him to the whole group.

questions.prompt: Which sentence keeps the verb tenses correct? 'Last summer we ___ to the lake, and next summer we ___ there again.'
questions.hint: Match each verb to its time clue: 'last summer' already happened, 'next summer' has not happened yet.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Went, will go' is right. 'Went' is the past for last summer, 'will go' is the future for next summer. Perfect!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. One blank is about last summer and one is about next summer. Match each verb to its time.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'went, will go.' 'Went' shows the past for last summer, and 'will go' shows the future for next summer.

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate command of the conventions of standard English capitalization, punctuation, and spelling
standards.parent_tip: Quotation marks for talking, commas for lists and dates.

questions.prompt: Which uses quotation marks correctly?
questions.hint: Wrap the spoken words in quotation marks!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Maya said, "Hi!"' puts quotation marks around the exact words Maya spoke. You know your quotes!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Quotation marks wrap around the exact words a person says out loud. Which one does that best?
questions.reveal_feedback: The correct one is 'Maya said, "Hi!"' The quotation marks go around Hi, the exact word Maya spoke.

questions.prompt: What punctuation is needed in a date like **March 5 2026**?
choices: a comma between day and year
questions.correct: a comma between day and year
questions.hint: Always put a comma between the day and year!
questions.correct_feedback: Right! A date needs a comma between the day and the year, like March 5, 2026. Great punctuation eye!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about how you write a full date. What small mark goes between the day number and the year?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'a comma between day and year.' We write it March 5, 2026, with a comma before the year.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?
choices: I bought apples, bananas, and grapes.
I bought apples bananas and grapes.
I bought, apples, bananas, and, grapes.
I bought apples; bananas; and grapes.
questions.correct: I bought apples, bananas, and grapes.
questions.hint: Use commas to separate items in a list!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'I bought apples, bananas, and grapes' uses commas to separate the list. That's just right!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! When you list three things, commas go between the items. Which sentence spaces them out correctly?
questions.reveal_feedback: The correct sentence is 'I bought apples, bananas, and grapes.' Commas separate each item in the list.

questions.prompt: Which is **possessive** (shows owning)?
questions.hint: Apostrophe + S shows owning!
questions.correct_feedback: Right! 'The kid's bag' uses an apostrophe to show the bag belongs to the kid. You spotted the owning word!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. To show something belongs to one person, you add a special mark. Which choice has it?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'the kid's bag.' The apostrophe and s show the bag belongs to the kid.

questions.prompt: Where do quotation marks go in: She said hello?
questions.hint: Spoken words go inside quotation marks!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'She said, "hello."' wraps quotation marks around the spoken word and adds a comma. Nicely done!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. The quotation marks go around the exact word she said. Which one also has the right comma?
questions.reveal_feedback: The correct one is 'She said, "hello."' The comma comes after said, and quotes wrap the spoken word hello.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?
questions.hint: When a quote ends with a strong mark like ! , that mark stays inside the quotation marks and no extra comma is added before the speaker tag.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! '"I found it!" yelled Sam.' keeps the exclamation mark inside the quotes with Sam's exact words. Great eye!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The exclamation mark belongs inside the quotation marks with the words Sam yelled. Which one does that neatly?
questions.reveal_feedback: The correct one is '"I found it!" yelled Sam.' The exclamation mark stays inside the quotes with Sam's exact words.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct word: "The three ___ tails wagged happily."
questions.hint: There is more than one dog, and the tails belong to them. For a plural word that already ends in -s, add just an apostrophe after the s.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! 'Dogs'' shows the tails belong to more than one dog. The apostrophe goes after the s. Well done!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, there are three dogs, so it is many. Where does the apostrophe go when more than one owns something?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'dogs'.' When more than one dog owns the tails, the apostrophe goes after the s.

questions.prompt: Choose the word that correctly fills the blank: "The kitten curled up and licked ___ soft paws."
questions.hint: Try reading it as "it is" — if that doesn't make sense, you need the possessive form, which has no apostrophe.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Its' shows the paws belong to the kitten, with no apostrophe. That's the tricky one, and you got it!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, one spelling shows the paws BELONG to the kitten, and the other is a shortcut for two words. Which one shows belonging?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'its' with no apostrophe. It shows the paws belong to the kitten, while it's means it is.

questions.prompt: Which spelling correctly adds -ed to the word 'hop' in this sentence: "The frog ___ across the pond."
questions.hint: For a short-vowel word ending in one consonant, double the final consonant before adding -ed.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! 'Hopped' doubles the p before adding -ed. That short-vowel rule tripped up others, but not you!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! When a short word like hop ends in one vowel and one consonant, something happens to the last letter before -ed.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'hopped.' Because hop has a short vowel, we double the p before adding -ed.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses capital letters correctly?
choices: We visited Lake Michigan in July.
We Visited Lake Michigan in July.
We visited lake Michigan in july.
we visited Lake michigan in July.
questions.correct: We visited Lake Michigan in July.
questions.hint: Capitalize the specific name of a place and the name of a month, but not ordinary verbs like 'visited.'
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'We visited Lake Michigan in July' capitalizes the place name and the month, but not the little words. Perfect!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Names of places and months start with capital letters, but everyday words do not. Which sentence follows that?
questions.reveal_feedback: The correct one is 'We visited Lake Michigan in July.' Lake Michigan and July are names, so they get capitals.

standards.standard_description: Use knowledge of language and its conventions when writing, speaking, reading, or listening
standards.parent_tip: Pick words that match your audience and purpose.

questions.prompt: Which is the strongest way to say **good**?
questions.hint: Fantastic means really, really good!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Fantastic' is the strongest, most exciting way to say good. You picked the powerful word!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, some of these barely mean good at all. Which word sounds the biggest and most exciting?
questions.reveal_feedback: The strongest word is 'fantastic.' It means much more than fine or okay, it means really, really good.

questions.prompt: You are writing to a friend. Which is best?
questions.hint: Friends use casual greetings!
questions.correct_feedback: Right! Writing to a friend, 'Hey, what's up?' is friendly and just the right tone. Good sense of audience!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. You are writing to a friend, not a stranger. Which greeting sounds relaxed and friendly?
questions.reveal_feedback: The best one is 'Hey, what's up?' It's friendly and casual, which fits when you write to a friend.

questions.prompt: Which word adds the most picture to **walked**?
questions.hint: Strolled means walked in a relaxed way!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Strolled' shows a slow, easy walk, so you can picture it. That's a word that paints a picture!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Most of these just mean go. Which word shows a special way of walking that you can picture?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'strolled.' It paints a picture of a slow, relaxed walk, more than plain words like went or moved.

questions.prompt: Which sentence has the strongest verb?
choices: The cat pounced on the toy.
The cat went on the toy.
The cat sat on the toy.
The cat was on the toy.
questions.correct: The cat pounced on the toy.
questions.hint: Pounced shows action vividly!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Pounced' is a strong verb that shows the cat leaping. It gives you a clear action picture!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! A strong verb shows exciting action you can see. Which cat verb is full of energy?
questions.reveal_feedback: The strongest verb is 'pounced.' It shows the cat leaping with energy, much more than sat or was.

questions.prompt: A storm tore the roof clean off the barn. Which word best replaces "strong" in "The wind was strong" to show HOW powerful it really was?
questions.hint: Match the word to the clue in the sentence — the wind ripped a roof off. Which word carries that much force?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Fierce' shows wind powerful enough to tear off a roof. It matches the strength perfectly!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the wind ripped a roof clean off. Which word shows wind that wild and powerful, not gentle?
questions.reveal_feedback: The best word is 'fierce.' It shows wind wild and powerful enough to tear a roof off, unlike chilly or steady.

questions.prompt: You are writing a thank-you note to your school principal. Which sentence fits the situation best?
choices: The trip was lit, no cap.
Thank you for organizing our wonderful field trip.
Thanks a ton for the awesome trip!
questions.correct: Thank you for organizing our wonderful field trip.
questions.hint: Think about your audience. Would you talk to a principal the same way you text a friend?
questions.correct_feedback: Right! 'Thank you for organizing our wonderful field trip' is polite and fits writing to a principal. Great tone!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. You are writing to your principal, so slang doesn't fit. Which sentence sounds polite and respectful?
questions.reveal_feedback: The best one is 'Thank you for organizing our wonderful field trip.' It's polite and respectful, which fits a note to your principal.

questions.prompt: Marco had not eaten all day and was starving. Which word best completes the sentence for effect: "He ___ his sandwich in seconds."?
questions.hint: The most precise word should MATCH how hungry Marco was, not fight against it.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Devoured' shows Marco eating fast and hungrily, just right for someone starving. Powerful word choice!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Marco was starving and ate it in seconds. Which word shows big, hungry, fast eating, not tiny bites?
questions.reveal_feedback: The best word is 'devoured.' It shows fast, hungry eating, which fits Marco who hadn't eaten all day, unlike nibbled or tasted.

questions.prompt: Four words all describe being happy. Which one shows the SMALLEST, mildest amount of happiness?
questions.hint: Picture the words on a ladder from a little happy to bursting with happiness. Which sits on the bottom rung?
questions.correct_feedback: Right! 'Content' is a calm, gentle kind of happy, the mildest of them all. You felt the difference!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Some of these mean super, super happy. Which word shows the smallest, calmest bit of happiness?
questions.reveal_feedback: The mildest word is 'content.' It means quietly happy and satisfied, much gentler than thrilled or ecstatic.

questions.prompt: An author wants readers to feel uneasy about an old house. Which word should she choose to describe it?
questions.hint: Any of these could describe an old house — pick the one whose feeling matches the mood she wants.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Gloomy' gives the house a dark, uneasy feeling, exactly what the author wanted. Great mood match!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The author wants readers to feel uneasy and a little scared. Which word gives a dark, spooky feeling?
questions.reveal_feedback: The best word is 'gloomy.' It gives a dark, uneasy feeling, unlike cozy or peaceful, which feel safe and calm.
